ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_PANDA"   ;   Str = "Scientific name -- Ailuropoda melanoleuca\nSize -- up to 300 lbs.\nDiet -- mainly bamboo\n\nThe Chinese name for the panda, da xiong mao, means 'great bear-cat'. The panda is a member of the bear family, and generally resembles other bears in all but its unique black-and-white coloring. Its shaggy fur is white with black patches over the eyes, ears, and legs, and a black band across its shoulders. Pandas are native to the forests high in several mountain ranges in southwestern China, primarily in Sichuan, but also in neighboring provinces such as Shaanxi and Gansu.\n\nKnown for their distinctive black-and-white coloring, these bears have a highly specialized diet, primarily consuming bamboo, although they occasionally eat other available foods. Since its main source of food is the bamboo tree, a panda is specially suited to hold, crush, and then consume this tough plant. One of its unique features is an enlarged sesamoid bone located on the forefoot. This bone holds the nearest tendon slightly further away from the joint than is normal, increasing its movement and enabling a special action similar to that of an opposable thumb. This allows for dexterous grasping. The panda’s teeth and jaws are designed to handle the job of grinding down bamboo shoots. They are bigger and broader than those of other bears, and the jawbones and cheek muscles are incredibly powerful.\n\nPandas are typically solitary animals, spending most of their time eating, resting, and searching for more food. Many pandas live in captivity, and their popularity has raised awareness of the animal’s plight in the wild. An estimated 1,600 pandas still live in their natural habitat, but only 60% of them live in protected forests. Saving pandas in the wild has become an important goal for conservationists, one that has sparked the imagination and support of the public. The panda has become an icon for endangered animal causes worldwide."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_PIXIU"   ;   Str = "Scientific name: Vorator auri\nSize: 10' long, 600 lbs\nDiet: precious metals and stones.\n\nPixiu is a famous, auspicious, winged mythical creature in Chinese culture. It is widely believed that Pixiu can attract wealth and bring prosperity, helping people become wealthy."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_QILIN"   ;   Str = "Scientific name: Chimera sinensis\nSize: 12' long, 1000 lbs\nDiet: Herbivorous\n\nQilin is a mythical creature with a body resembling that of a horse with a horn on its head. It is said that its appearance heralds a golden age or the arrival of a sage, making it a symbol of good fortune in China.\n\nAlthough the Qilin's description may resemble that of unicorns in other mythologies, the giraffes from Africa presented to Chinese emperors were referred to as Qilin. In fact, in Japanese, both giraffes and Qilin are still called 'Kirin' to this day."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_QINGHAILAKESHRINE"   ;   Str = "Qinghai Lake, the largest lake in China, is a location steeped in mythology. Located in Northwest China, the lake was originally a shrine. The White Dragon, also known as Ao Run or Báilóng, is considered the patron of the lake. One legend suggests that the White Dragon created the lake after diverting water from over one hundred other lakes. Another legend states the deity Xiwangmu used the lake to host beautiful banquets for her fellow deities. Either way, the lake holds special significance in Chinese mythological memory."

It is celebrated for its elegant appearance and is a symbol of luck, longevity, and fidelity. These cranes are known for their striking red patch on the crown, which contrasts with their primarily white plumage. They inhabit wetlands and riverbanks, where they can be seen performing complex mating dances."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_SAGE"   ;   Str = "In Chinese belief systems, the sage was one of the most respected figures: regarded as a beacon of wisdom and nobility, and venerated for their virtue and scholarly ability. There are many notable sages throughout Chinese history, such as the Four Sages of the Confucian Tradition (Yan Hui, Mengzi, Zisi, and Mencius) and the Five Sage Emperors.\n\nWith their persuasive eloquence, sages can charm enemy creatures. Additionally, they often rely on their esteemed reputation to bolster troop morale and heal comrades, making them the most respected individuals in the camp."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_SILO"   ;   Str = "The Silo serves as the Chinese dropsite for resources.\n\nGrain was essential for maintaining the emperor’s rule in ancient China. For a long time, farmers paid the government not with currency, but with various types of produce, with grain being the most important. To secure these resources, large silos were built across China, storing enough food to last several years or even over a decade. Many large-scale battles centered around these silos; defenders could endure prolonged sieges with their stored grain, while attackers, upon capturing a silo, often saw surrounding forces surrender for survival. These massive silos symbolized control and dominance over the region."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_SKYLANTERN"   ;   Str = "Sky lanterns, also known as Kongming lanterns, are ancient Chinese paper lanterns. According to legend, they were invented by the renowned military strategist Zhuge Liang, also known as Kongming. It is said he used them as distress signals when surrounded by enemies, which is why they are also called Kongming lanterns.\n\nIn addition to distress signals, lanterns are also utilized for making wishes and transmitting messages. In some legends, people even made large sky lanterns to illuminate the path for armies at night."

Recognizable by their bright, white spots on a reddish-brown coat, these deer are prevalent in wooded regions of India and Sri Lanka. They are highly social animals, often found in large herds, and are primarily grass grazers, though they will seek out other plants if grass is not available."

ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_TRANSPORTSHIPCHINESE"   ;   Str = "Like other civilizations, China began using boats early in its history and constructed numerous canals across the country to facilitate transportation. ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_VILLAGERCHINESE"   ;   Str = "Farming and laboring peasants formed the vast majority of ancient China’s population. However, these villages were vulnerable to natural disasters like droughts and river floods, and farmers were often conscripted for large-scale projects to build or improve irrigation canals and dams."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_VILLAGERCHINESECLAY"   ;   Str = "In the beginning, only the heaven and the earth existed. All the clay that fell off the cord became peasants.\n\nSome legends suggest that the men and women Nüwa molded by hand became the noble class, whereas the ones who fell off the cord became the working class."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_WENZHONG"   ;   Str = "Age: 2300 years\nHomeland: Jizhou, Mythical China\nOccupation: warlord and strategist\n\nWen Zhong is a character in the Chinese classical mythological novel 'The Investiture of the Gods.' In battles against the Zhou dynasty, he defeated Jiang Ziya several times but ultimately met his demise. After Wen Zhong's death, Jiang Ziya bestowed upon him the title of Thunder God.\n\nHere is the decree from Jiang Ziya in the novel:\n\n“You, Wen Zhong, once entered the famous mountains and cultivated the Great Dao. Although you attained the fruit of returning to the source, you have not yet realized the ultimate truth of oneness. You are destined not to reach the highest realm of immortals but held the highest rank of a minister, assisting two dynasties with utmost loyalty. Though your fate was sealed by the calamities of the era, your steadfastness and integrity are admirable. Now, I appoint you to oversee the Thunder Division, to summon clouds and bring rain, nurturing all living things, punishing the wicked and rewarding the good. I hereby confer upon you the title of Nine Heavens Responder to the Primordial Thunder God, Universal Transformer, and Supreme Sovereign. You shall lead the 24 Thunder Division Heavenly Lords, who assist in cloud summoning and rainmaking, and enforce the law. Fulfill your duties diligently.”"

ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_FIREARCHER"   ;   Str = "The Chinese were the first to invent gunpowder; however, at first the inventors were unsure of how to implement the substance in warfare. Evidence suggests that fire arrows, along with firebombs, were among the first ever gunpowder weapons used in battle.\n\nEven before the invention of fire arrows, the Chinese were renowned archers, and they placed significant emphasis on the practice. For example, archery was one of the Six Noble Arts of the Zhou dynasty. The mythological hero Houyi was also a renowned archer, famous for shooting nine of the ten suns of Chinese mythology. Archery remained a relevant skill in Chinese culture for centuries."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_TERRACOTTARIDER"   ;   Str = "The Terracotta Rider is a member of the Terracotta Army, sworn to protect Emperor Qin Shi Huang. They were forerunners, the first great ones, overcome by their offspring and cast into the remoteness of Tartarus.\n\nThey governed phenomena and concepts like time, daring, and the unseen, as well as forces of nature like the Sun, Earth and Sky. ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_PEACHBLOSSOMSPRING"   ;   Str = "The Peach Blossom Spring is based on a famous essay by Tao Qian, written in 421 CE, that tells the story of a fisherman who discovered a mythical grove of peach blossoms:\n\n“One day, as he followed the course of a stream, he became unconscious of the distance he had travelled. All at once he came upon a grove of blossoming peach trees which lined either bank for hundreds of paces. No tree of any other kind stood amongst them, but there were fragrant flowers, delicate and lovely to the eye, and the air was with drifting peach bloom.”"

His contributions greatly benefited the health and prosperity of all Chinese people."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_GREATWALL"   ;   Str = "The Great Wall was constructed over nearly 2,000 years, with significant construction beginning in the 7th century BCE under the state of Chu. Over the following centuries, neighboring states adopted similar efforts. Built to protect China from northern invaders, the wall extends across northern China and southern Mongolia.\n\nThe best-preserved section of the wall, built during the Ming Dynasty (1368–1644 CE), spans over 5,000 miles (8,000 km). In total, the wall stretches more than 13,000 miles (21,000 km). It was named a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1987."

ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_TAICHI" ; Str = "One of the primary principles of Tai Chi is the flow between Yin and Yang. Fuxi and his sister-wife Nüwa traditionally represent this flow between hard (Yang) and soft (Yin), respectively. Another such principle is the conquering of the unyielding with the yielding: under the correct circumstances, softness can be used to conquer toughness."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_LASTSTAND" ; Str = "In an attempt to decide who was the strongest god, Gonggong challenged Zhurong to a major battle. The two gods fought in the heavens for days, until Zhurong defeated Gonggong and threw him to the earth. Despite his defeat at Zhurong's hands, Gonggong still had some fight left in him: he took out Mount Buzhou, which brought cosmic disruption to China."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_RISINGTIDE" ; Str = "Gonggong was a god of chaos; once, in a fit of rage, he famously caused a massive flood by bumping into Mount Buzhou. The rising tides spelled disaster for the Chinese people, leading to Gonggong’s demise."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_SHAKEROFHEAVEN" ; Str = "When Gonggong crashed into Mount Buzhou, the heavens literally shook and the sky fell. Mount Buzhou was one of the four pillars that upheld the heavens – when Gonggong took it down, the sky collapsed. The goddess Nüwa was forced to repair the heavens with five colorful rocks that she melted together."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_TEMPESTUOUSSTORM" ; Str = "One of Gonggong's major defining features is his depiction as a tempestuous storm god: when he engaged in battle, he caused flooding and cosmic chaos."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_HOOVESOFTHEWIND" ; Str = "Goumang’s appearance is deeply associated with air: he is usually depicted with the body of a bird and the face of a human. He also rides two dragons through the air whenever he is traveling."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_FRENZIEDDASH" ; Str = "One of the 'Four Perils' or 'Four Great Malevolent Beasts' in Chinese legend, the Taowu is similar in size to a tiger, covered in dog fur, and has sharp fangs.\n\nThe Taowu, with its muscular frame and four powerful legs, is an exceptionally fast creature. It is also highly prone to frenzied outbursts of violence and brutality."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_MAELSTROM" ; Str = "Goumang, also known as Jumang, is the ancient Chinese deity of wood and spring. He is responsible for overseeing plant growth and is credited with inventing the luo, a net used for catching birds. In addition to presiding over spring and agriculture, he serves as the guardian god of the East.\n\nIn ancient texts, Goumang is depicted with a human face and a bird's body, riding two dragons. In some stories, he also appears as a cowherd boy riding a buffalo."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_VIBRANTLAND" ; Str = "“The East belongs to Wood. Its god is Taihao. His subordinate is Goumang. Both of them hold the compasses and control the season of spring.” - Huainanzi"
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_ABUNDANCE" ; Str = "Hou means ‘divine sovereign’ and tu means ‘earth’ – it was sometimes used as a term of respect for the physical earth, but it is most frequently utilized as a name for the female earth deity.\n\nPeople prayed to Houtu for abundance frequently, whether that came in the form of an abundant harvest, a large rainfall, multiple children, vast wealth, or overall health."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_ADVANCEDDEFENSES" ; Str = "In some mythological texts, Houtu was the ruler of both the earth and the netherworld, known as Youdu. Houtu had an assistant named Tubo, who guarded the gates to Youdu. Thus, Houtu and Tubo both possessed great defensive knowledge, as it was essential that they guard the netherworld from those who would try to infiltrate it or escape it."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_CHASINGTHESUN" ; Str = "Kuafu is a giant in Chinese mythology known for his immense size and incredible speed. He is most famous for his feat of chasing the sun. Overconfident in his own strength and speed, he attempted to catch the sun but grew thirsty in the process. He drank entire rivers and marshes, yet could not quench his thirst. Ultimately, he died from dehydration.\n\nThe idiom ‘chasing the sun’ in Chinese is used to describe someone who is overly ambitious or extremely determined. It can be used positively or negatively."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_SINISTERDEFIANCE" ; Str = "One of the 'Four Perils' or 'Four Great Malevolent Beasts' in Chinese legend, Qiongqi has the appearance of a winged tiger and understands human language. The Qiongqi is known for its appetite for human flesh.\n\nQiongqi's sense of justice is opposite to that of humans. It enjoys arbitrating human disputes by devouring the righteous party and rewarding the wicked. To punish trustworthy individuals, the Qiongqi is known to eat their noses."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_IMPERIALORDER" ; Str = "Huangdi did not enter into war lightly: he was cherished for prioritizing the wellbeing of his people over conquest. However, when he did enter into conflict, he came like a storm: the men that Huangdi called to fight up for him fought bravely, because they believed in his vision for a better world."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_POWEROFCHAOS" ; Str = "One of the 'Four Perils' or 'Four Great Malevolent Beasts' in Chinese legend, the Hundun is a chaotic, faceless being. It has four wings and six feet, but is typically a slow moving beast. The creature shares similarities with the Dijiang from the Classic of Mountains and Seas."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_LEIZUSSILK" ; Str = "Leizu is revered as the goddess of sericulture, the art and process of silk production. After meeting and marrying Huangdi, she taught his people how to raise silkworms and weave silk fabric.\n\nThere are various myths about how she discovered the art of silk weaving. According to one version, she ate fruit from a mulberry tree and began vomiting silk threads. She then used leaves from the same tree to feed moths, which transformed into silkworms.\n\nLeizu remains an important figure in China and is still worshipped by sericulturists. A temple dedicated to her in Yuan’an County, Hubei Province, commemorates her significant contributions to Chinese culture and life."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_XUANYUANSBLOODLINE" ; Str = "Huangdi, who is also named Xuanyuan, is widely worshipped as the common ancestor of the ancient Chinese people.\n\nOne scholarly mythological text lists a number of Huangdi’s most prominent descendants, “such as Gun and Yu, the great heroes who controlled the flood; Zhuanxu, the mythical emperor; Zhong and Li, the heroes separating heaven from earth; Shujun, the Cultivation God; and Yuqiang, the Sea God.” -The Handbook of Chinese Mythology, Lihui Yang and Deming An."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_BOTTOMLESSSTOMACH" ; Str = "One of the 'Four Perils' or 'Four Great Malevolent Beasts' in Chinese legend, Taotie has an appearance similar to that of a sheep and symbolizes unending greed.\n\nTaotie's insatiable appetite makes it a symbol of war and slaughter."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_DROUGHTSHIPS" ; Str = "Nüba is the goddess of drought. She possesses the power to dry up the land and prevent rainfall.\n\nIt is said that wherever Nüba lives, drought follows; because of this, she often moved from place to place. There is evidence of multiple ritual customs across China where they would “drive Nüba away”."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_ROCKSOLID" ; Str = "Because Nüba is the goddess of drought, she is associated with rock solid land.\n\nShe also played a pivotal role as a warrior in the battle between Huangdi and Chiyou. When Chiyou enlisted the rain god Yu Shi to aid him, Huangdi summoned his daughter, Nüba, for assistance. Nüba intervened by drying up all the rain, tipping the scales in Huangdi’s favor and ultimately securing his victory."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_SCORCHINGFEATHERS" ; Str = "Another side effect of drought was scorching heat, drying up the land and allowing for fire to flourish. Thus, Nüba’s abilities pair beautifully with a fire attack, leaving her enemies unable to escape the blistering heat."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_KUAFUCHIEFTAIN" ; Str = "Kuafu is a giant in Chinese mythology known for his immense size and incredible speed. He is most famous for his feat of chasing the sun. Overconfident in his own strength and speed, he attempted to catch the sun but grew thirsty in the process. He drank entire rivers and marshes, yet could not quench his thirst. Ultimately, he died from dehydration.\n\nAccording to some scholars, Kuafu may have been an ancient tribe in China from which the mythological giant received his name."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_MOUNTAINOUSMIGHT" ; Str = "After Gonggong smashed into Mount Suzhou and broke the four pillars supporting the sky, Nüwa used her immense strength to repair the damage. She melted five colored stones to patch the sky, cut off the legs of a gigantic tortoise to replace the broken pillars, and ultimately battled a black dragon to save her people.\n\nThis famous myth highlights Nüwa’s extraordinary physical strength as well as her other remarkable qualities."

ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_REDCLIFFSFLEET" ; Str = "The Battle of Red Cliffs is one of the most famous naval battles in Chinese history. Fought during the Three Kingdoms period, the battle pitted three major warlords against one another: on one side, Cao Cao, who fought against an alliance between Liu Bei and Sun Quan. Despite being vastly outnumbered, Liu Bei and Sun Quan outmaneuvered Cao Cao’s massive army by using a clever fire attack that reduced his fleet to ashes."

ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_HERBALMEDICINE" ; Str = "Perhaps Shennong’s greatest contribution to the Chinese people was the knowledge of herbal medicine. He famously tested hundreds of plants and herbs for their medicinal qualities, once consuming seventy poisonous plants in one day. He is credited as the author of one of the first Chinese medical texts, Shennong Bencao Jing (Classic of Shennong’s Materia Medica)."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_PEACHOFIMMORTALITY" ; Str = "The peach of immortality, or pántáo, was a fruit that grew in Xiwangmu’s garden. According to some legends, the fruit ripened only once every 3,000 years. When it did, immortals would gather to partake in a grand celebration known as the Feast of Peaches. It was also believed that any mortal who ate one of these peaches would attain immortality."

It is said that its appearance heralds a golden age or the arrival of a sage.\n\nBecause of this, the Qilin is deeply associated with periods of peace and prosperity for the Chinese people. "

ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_SLASHANDBURN" ; Str = "Slash-and-burn agriculture was developed in ancient China during the Neolithic period. This practice utilized fire, Zhurong’s domain, to fertilize the soil for farming. The ancient Chinese cut down sections of forest and burned them, using the ashes as fertilizer for crops such as rice and corn."

ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_CONSCRIPTBAOLEISOLDIERS" ; Str = "The last resort for a ruler needing soldiers was the forced enlistment of available able-bodied men into military service. This compulsory draft of manpower, or conscription, was disruptive and unpleasant, and a step not taken lightly. Unless conscripts were well led and highly motivated, they usually performed poorly – but conscription did put soldiers into the field quickly, and this could make the difference in an emergency."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_CROSSBOWTOWER" ; Str = "The crossbow has a history of several thousand years in China. The Chu Ko Nu (or Zhuge Nu) repeating crossbow was known for its simple design and incredible rate of fire. It held a magazine of roughly 10 bolts which it shot in succession, and was operated by grasping the stock in the left hand and working the lever with the right. In that swift movement, a bolt would drop into place, the string would be drawn, and then the bolt would be fired, with another bolt dropping in to take its place.\n\n
The Chu Ko Nu is first attested in the 4th century BCE, when it was used in combat during the Warring States Period. However, legend holds that it was invented by the renowned ancient Chinese military strategist Zhuge Liang. Regardless of the weapon's actual provenance, an army equipped with them could be a nightmare for enemies."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_DIVINELIGHT" ; Str = "Sky lanterns, also known as Kongming lanterns, are ancient Chinese paper lanterns. According to legend, they were invented by the renowned military strategist Zhuge Liang, also known as Kongming. It is said he used them as distress signals when surrounded by enemies, which is why they are also called Kongming lanterns.\n\nIn addition to distress signals, lanterns are also utilized for making wishes and transmitting messages. In some legends, people even made large sky lanterns to illuminate the path for armies at night."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_EASTWIND" ; Str = "When Zhuge Liang, the famous inventor of the Sky Lantern, fought at the Battle of Red Cliffs (Chi Bi), he harnessed the power of the east wind to help set the warlord Cao Cao’s fleet ablaze."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_FISHBASKET" ; Str = "Fishing has always been an important source of sustenance in East Asia, spurring a constant drive to improve technologies related to fishing boats, nets, navigation, and traps. Evidence of these fishing technologies has been unearthed at sites dating back six thousand years. Legend states that fish baskets were invented by the god Fuxi as a gift to humans."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_GREATWALL" ; Str = "The Great Wall was constructed over nearly 2,000 years, with significant construction beginning in the 7th century BCE under the state of Chu. Over the following centuries, neighboring states adopted similar efforts. Built to protect China from northern invaders, the wall extends across northern China and southern Mongolia.\n\nThe best-preserved section of the wall, built during the Ming Dynasty (1368–1644 CE), spans over 5,000 miles (8,000 km). In total, the wall stretches more than 13,000 miles (21,000 km). It was named a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1987."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_LEVYBAOLEISOLDIERS" ; Str = "Where a population had a large percentage of non-slaves, the government usually made provision to mobilize the free men for military service. Citizens always available on standby in emergencies were called the levy. The ruler could call out the levy under certain conditions, but especially if enemies were approaching. The levy was a formal understanding and obligation, roughly equivalent to a tax or mandatory public service. Greek adult freemen, for example, were expected to serve in their town’s levy."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_SILKROAD" ; Str = "The Silk Road was a trade route that connected China to the Western world. It originated in the 2nd century BCE and remained in use until the 15th century CE.\n\nA wide variety of goods were exchanged along this route. The Chinese exported silk, tea, dyes, porcelain, and other items west in return for items such as wool, gold, silver, and horses. Beyond trade, the Silk Road facilitated the exchange of ideas, better connecting the world."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_SKYFIRE" ; Str = "Sky lanterns, also known as Kongming lanterns, are ancient Chinese paper lanterns. According to legend, they were invented by the renowned military strategist Zhuge Liang, also known as Kongming. It is said he used them as distress signals when surrounded by enemies, which is why they are also called Kongming lanterns.\n\n
In addition to distress signals, lanterns are also utilized for making wishes and transmitting messages. In some legends, people even made large sky lanterns to illuminate the path for armies at night. When Zhuge Liang, fought at the Battle of Red Cliffs (Chi Bi), he harnessed the power of the east wind to help set the warlord Cao Cao’s fleet ablaze."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_BROKENCELESTIALPILLAR" ; Str = "After Pangu separated Heaven and Earth, the Celestial Pillars were formed to uphold the heavens and prevent the worlds from collapsing into one another. Each pillar stands at one of the cardinal directions.\n\nThe Northern Pillar – Located in the far north, amidst vast pine forests and towering mountains.\n\nThe Eastern Pillar – Positioned along the coastline, surrounded by deciduous forests, fertile farmlands, bustling urban centers, and mighty rivers.\n\nThe Western Pillar – Situated on the empire's western edge, in the driest region of China, characterized by deserts and arid vegetation.\n\nThe Southern Pillar – Found in the south, encircled by numerous waterways and expansive, lush bamboo forests."

The divine god Shennong urged him to find a champion to save the people of China.\n\nAt times, he doubts his mission and fears the worst outcomes, but his unwavering faith in the will of the gods empowers him to endure hardships."

These stones are:\n\nThe Blue Stone – Representing wood, it is a fragment of Yggdrasil’s roots, the world tree, from Niflheim, the Norse underworld.\n\nThe Red Stone – Representing fire, it is found within the lava pits of Erebus, the Greek underworld.\n\nThe Yellow Stone – Representing earth, it is sacred soil revered in Egypt for its immense fertility.\n\nThe White Stone – Representing metal, it is a material used to forge many celestial weapons wielded by gods and heroes.\n\nThe Black Stone – Representing water, it contains the essence of corals found only in the submerged lands of Atlantis."

It is mounted on a specialized launching platform or war chariot to fire large arrows and typically consists of multiple bows working in unison.\n\nAccording to historical records, this type of crossbow had a maximum range of up to 1.5 kilometers, with one instance where it successfully killed an enemy commander from 400 meters away."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_GREATWALLGATE" ; Str = "Walls prevent movement of any ground based units. Only flying units may cross Walls, although friendly units may pass through Walls at Gates.\n\nThe Great Wall was constructed over nearly 2,000 years, with significant construction beginning in the 7th century BCE under the state of Chu. Over the following centuries, neighboring states adopted similar efforts. Built to protect China from northern invaders, the wall extends across northern China and southern Mongolia.\n\nThe best-preserved section of the wall, built during the Ming Dynasty (1368–1644 CE), spans over 5,000 miles (8,000 km). In total, the wall stretches more than 13,000 miles (21,000 km). It was named a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1987."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_HARPYSPC" ; Str = "The harpy is a mythological creature that is typically depicted with the face of a woman and the wings of a bird. Their birdlike appearance may derive from their being considered the personification of storm clouds and fierce winds. They are described as “evil” in texts like the Aeneid and the story of Jason and the Argonauts, and it is said that they were hideous to look at and had an unclean odor. "

ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_MECHANICALOXSPC" ; Str = "Trade was critical to the survival of any ancient empire, as different geographic areas often had resources in abundance while others were sorely lacking. Cultivated silk production began in China thousands of years ago and became a staple of westward trade for millennia. However, traversing vast territories posed significant challenges. During times of war, transporting supplies from the fertile southwestern basins to other regions was always a major obstacle for the Chinese. To address this, the great military strategist Zhuge Liang invented the 'Mechanical Ox Caravan', a tool designed for efficient transport along mountain paths.\n\nIt is said that this mechanical device could easily traverse uphill and downhill slopes, continuously delivering supplies to Zhuge Liang's northern campaign front."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_MONUMENTTOPHARAOHSSPC" ; Str = "Monuments are special buildings available to Egyptian players only. There are five different monuments, each of increasingly large size and cost. Each monument provides a continual stream of Favor. Players can build all five monuments to obtain a maximum income of Favor from their gods.\n\nAncient Egypt was a wealthy civilization largely isolated from external enemies, which allowed lavish expenditures for Monuments and other edifices that glorified gods and pharaohs. For several thousand years, major construction projects were almost continually underway, for the glorification of some god or ruler. The Nile Valley is still dotted with monuments of all sizes. The most famous of these are the Great Pyramids of Giza and the Great Sphinx."

ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_PENGLAI" ; Str = "Penglai Island is a mythical island in ancient Chinese legends, said to be located overseas in the East Sea and surrounded by a black mist sea.\n\nIt is described in the short story the 'Record of the Peach Blossom Spring' written by the poet Tao Yuanming (365-427 CE). The story describes the adventure of an ordinary fisherman who accidentally enters a secluded and isolated place:\n\n“One day, as he followed the course of a stream, he became unconscious of the distance he had travelled. All at once he came upon a grove of blossoming peach trees which lined either bank for hundreds of paces. No tree of any other kind stood amongst them, but there were fragrant flowers, delicate and lovely to the eye, and the air was filled with drifting peach bloom.”"
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_ROCKINVISIBLE" ; Str = "This rock is not natural. A mysterious spell lies upon it."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_TEMPLECHINESESPC" ; Str = "Mount Buzhou holds great significance in Chinese mythology as one of the celestial sky pillars that support the heavens above the earth. Located north of the Kunlun Mountains, it became a pivotal site during the war between Gonggong and Zhurong. In the conflict, Gonggong collided with Mount Buzhou, shattering the cords that stabilized the earth. This calamity compelled Nüwa to repair the pillars to restore balance and prevent cosmological chaos."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_TITANGATESPC" ; Str = "The Titans are bound in Tartarus, or they were...\n\nTartarus was a prison for creatures distasteful to the reigning gods. When Oranos ruled the cosmos, he locked many of his unsightly children in the depths of Tartarus. When the Olympians rose to power, they threw the deposed Titans into Tartarus and bound them there with gates wrought of adamantine. The Titans spent millennia trying to find ways out of their prison.\n\nThis gate is a breach in that deep, dark prison and the Titans await below. Task Sages to empower the gate, and a Titan will emerge."

ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_WUZU_JAVELINEER" ; Str = "The Wuzu Guards were renowned elite infantry units of the Wei state during China’s Warring States period. At this time, China was divided into several powerful states, with Wei located in the central region. Due to the strength of the Wuzu Guards, the Wei state was able to maintain its dominance for multiple generations of rulers.\n\nAccording to an ancient Chinese source:\n\n“The Wuzu of the Wei clan were selected based on their stature. They wore triple-layered armor, wielded crossbows capable of shooting twelve-stone arrows, carried fifty arrows on their backs, and mounted a ge on top. They wore helmets, carried swords, and bore three days' worth of food. By midday, they could march a hundred li (approximately 50 kilometers).”"
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_XIAOLIHERO" ; Str = "Age – 40\nHomeland – China\nOccupation – wife to Huang Zhaowu\n\nXiaoli, the wife of Huang Zhaowu, was a mortal woman, unlike her husband. Her life was once filled with love and devotion to him, but after her death, she found herself in the underworld.\n\nDespite her new, shadowy existence, her loyalty to Huang Zhaowu remains unwavering, even as his actions grow darker and more twisted. Her steadfast loyalty, transcending life and death, drives her to remain by his side, even as his wickedness consumes him."

Observant, curious, and quietly competent, he has made it his goal to do everything in his power to help his newfound team succeed.\n\nWill Yan Feifeng evolve into the heroic, revered champion of the people and the empire’s guardian, as the gods intend him to?"

// Code of Conduct sections - https://www.ageofempires.com/code-of-conduct/

ID = "STR_CODE_OF_CONDUCT_CONTENTS_0"   ;   Str = "At World’s Edge, we pride ourselves on telling the stories of civilizations all throughout history and all throughout the world. We have our global community in mind when we build our games. We’re honored to have such a welcoming, friendly, and fair community of players.\n\nBuilding that community means trusting each other to embrace certain values that make all the Age of Empires games and social spaces safe and welcoming places to engage. Our platform services respect the fundamental rights of all parties involved, including your freedom of expression. This Code of Conduct and the Xbox Community Standards apply to anyone playing an Age of Empires game or engaging in an official social spaces."

ID = "STR_CODE_OF_CONDUCT_HEADER_1"   ;   Str = "We welcome new players."
ID = "STR_CODE_OF_CONDUCT_CONTENTS_1"   ;   Str = "New players are always discovering Age of Empires, which means our games have players of all skill levels. While competition on the battlefield can sometimes be fierce, harassment or abuse of others is unacceptable. When meeting a new player, take the time to make them feel welcome."

ID = "STR_CODE_OF_CONDUCT_HEADER_2"   ;   Str = "We build an inclusive community."
ID = "STR_CODE_OF_CONDUCT_CONTENTS_2"   ;   Str = "The Age of Empires franchise celebrates stories from civilizations all over the world, and our players come from every corner of the map as well! We learn from each other and celebrate our differences. Our team’s mission is to create safe spaces for our players to meet, game, discuss and engage. Prejudice against someone because of their gender, age, race, sexuality, nationality, ethnicity, or disability will not be tolerated."

ID = "STR_CODE_OF_CONDUCT_HEADER_3"   ;   Str = "We play fair and respect each other."
ID = "STR_CODE_OF_CONDUCT_CONTENTS_3"   ;   Str = "We play together and engage in respectful behavior even while competing. For example, saying “Good game” or “gg” after a competitive match. Engaging in behaviors to purposefully undermine matches goes against the spirit of the game. This can include, but is not limited to, repeatedly quitting a match early, sending excessive in-game taunts or tampering with the game.  This does not necessarily apply to various in-game strategies, for example tower rushing, which are part of the competitive nature of our games."

ID = "STR_CODE_OF_CONDUCT_HEADER_4"   ;   Str = "We keep each other safe."
ID = "STR_CODE_OF_CONDUCT_CONTENTS_4"   ;   Str = "We want players from all backgrounds to feel safe in our games.  This means being a force for good in-game and on any Age platforms. For example, cautioning another player if their behavior does not adhere to the Xbox Community Standards or this Code, or promoting constructive conversations on Age platforms. We encourage players to report behavior that impacts the safety and enjoyment of the game for themselves and others."

ID = "STR_CODE_OF_CONDUCT_HEADER_5"   ;   Str = "How can I help keep Age of Empires a welcoming place?"
ID = "STR_CODE_OF_CONDUCT_CONTENTS_5"   ;   Str = "We count on each other to report behavior that makes ourselves or others feel uncomfortable or unsafe. If you experience or witness behavior that does not support our values, we encourage you to mute or block the player and report the behavior.  Our human moderation team reviews reports of harmful behavior. Automated detection is used for gameplay behaviors and at the time content is uploaded. Moderation is applied diligently, objectively, and proportionately.\n\nIf there are additional details to include with a report, please reach out to our Support team with details of the violation.\n\nSteps for reporting harmful behavior in each game can be found in the Player Safety center."

// Japanese History
ID = "STR_HISTORY_JAPANESE" ; Str = "The Japanese scout with Kitsune myth units. \n\nJapanese soldiers tend to be versatile and powerful, but expensive. Their elite units from the Dojo are especially dangerous and contribute to a Bushidō meter which yields other bonuses. \n\nThe Japanese field a variety of ranged, mounted, and infantry heroes from their different military buildings. \n\nThe Japanese gain Favor by having Mikos build and tend to Shrines."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_YAS"   ;   Str = "This campaign follows Yasuko, the daughter of a humble farmer, who is thrust into a conflict far greater than herself when she is gifted a magical spear.\n\nIn the quiet village of Izumi, Yasuko encounters a wounded warrior during a journey into the nearby forest. When she returns, she finds her village under siege—and in the chaos, her father is kidnapped. Determined to save him, Yasuko sets off with her allies, only to be swept into a much larger struggle between gods and their mortal pawns. Will Yasuko grow from a meek farm girl into a steadfast warrior capable of saving all of Japan?\n\nIn this campaign, you will play as the Japanese."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_BUSHIDO"   ;   Str = "The Japanese utilize the unique Bushidō Code, reflecting a deep mastery of weaponry by their human infantry, ranged soldiers, and cavalry.\n\nBushidō's multiple tiers can be reached by earning combat experience. All Japanese human soldiers and heroes earn experience as they damage enemy units, with Dojo units doing so most rapidly. Dojo units also automatically earn a small amount of experience when not fighting.\n\nReaching each new Bushidō tier grants another level (Medium, Heavy, or Champion) of human unit upgrades for free at each Japanese military production building (Dojo, Guardhouse, and Stable). Progressing towards the next Bushidō tier also proportionally lowers the price of the next level of human unit upgrades, allowing Japanese players to research them at a reduced cost.\n\nThe Dojo has a final unique human unit upgrade beyond Champion that can only be attained via the Bushidō Code.\n\nEach Japanese Major God has a unique bonus connected to each Bushidō tier in addition to the shared effect of granting unit upgrades."

// Japanese God Power History
ID = "STR_HISTORY_GODPOWER_SOLAR_SHIELD"   ;   Str = "Amaterasu's God Power grants a single friendly unit a powerful regenerative shield. The shield provides a large amount of extra hitpoints and constantly heals the shielded unit, making it temporarily nearly invulnerable. Use Solar Shield to turn the tide of a battle by strengthening a key unit to lead a charge, or cast it on a grievously wounded unit to save it from destruction. Be aware that the effect will not last forever.\n\nTo shield a unit, select the Solar Shield icon at the top of the screen, then select your unit."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_GODPOWER_KUSANAGI"   ;   Str = "Susanoo's God Power, Kusanagi, sends his divine sword down to earth to strike the targeted location, dealing a chunk of damage to enemies on impact.\n\nThe sword then summons a churning riptide that continuously damages any foes caught in the water. After a moment, the riptide erupts in a single, massive updraft, launching all affected units skyward.\n\n\n\nTo call Susanoo's sword down upon your enemies, select the Kusanagi icon at the top of the screen, then select a location on the map to target."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_GODPOWER_NEW_MOON"   ;   Str = "Tsukuyomi's God Power blesses a building, causing it to train military units very quickly for a short duration and automatically researching all available technologies. Note that it will only research all technologies available at the time of its casting; for example, if you are in the Heroic Age but have not researched Copper Weapons yet, and you target an Armory, Copper Weapons – but not Bronze Weapons – will be researched.\n\nTo bless your building, select the New Moon icon, and then select the desired building."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_GODPOWER_GOSHINBOKU"   ;   Str = "Ame-no-Uzume's God Power causes a sacred Goshinboku tree to grow from the earth. This tree may be placed on any open area and provides a powerful blessing to whichever player controls it, resurrecting their fallen myth units for free.\n\nWhen a myth unit dies, its spirit travels to the Goshinboku to be reborn. The tree resurrects one unit at a time, always starting with the most recently fallen. It can hold a second spirit in its queue, but if a third myth unit perishes while the queue is full, its spirit is lost forever. The Goshinboku's power is great, but it cannot revive Kitsunes, lesser 'token' units, or powerful beings of divine might.\n\nTo plant the Goshinboku, select the God Power icon and then click a target location. Be warned: if an enemy defeats your nearby forces, they can seize control of the tree and claim its life-giving benefits for themselves."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_GODPOWER_SWAMPLAND"   ;   Str = "Inari Ōkami's God Power, Swampland, floods an area, creating a temporary, primordial marsh. This swamp is not just difficult terrain - it is a predator's lair.\n\nKappa lie hidden beneath the murky water. When an enemy soldier strays too close, a Kappa will erupt from the swamp to drag the unfortunate victim to a watery grave. Each Kappa can strike multiple times, so the only way to escape the slaughter is to retreat from the cursed ground. This makes the swamp a lethal trap for unwary armies.\n\n\n\nTo summon the Swampland, select the God Power icon, and then select the area where you want it to appear."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_GODPOWER_SWAMPLAND2"   ;   Str = "Inari Ōkami's God Power causes a swamp to manifest in the targeted area. The swamp slows down enemy units and boosts the gather rate of Farms in the vicinity, but only lasts for a short time.\n\nTo summon the swamp, select the Swampland icon, and then select the area where you want it to appear."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_GODPOWER_SHRINE_OF_THE_HUNT"   ;   Str = "Minakatatomi's God Power transforms one of your Shrines into the mystical Shrine of the Hunt.\n\nThe Shrine of the Hunt periodically summons friendly wolves to guard its grounds and wild deer, presenting a strategic choice: hunt the deer for a steady source of food, or leave them alive near the shrine to boost its Favor generation. These animals will replenish over time, and if the shrine is ever destroyed, any it summoned will remain. It also conveniently functions as a food drop-off point, unlike usual shrines.\n\nTo create a Shrine of the Hunt, select the God Power icon, and then select one of your Shrines."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_GODPOWER_SMITING_GUST"   ;   Str = "Fūjin's God Power, Smiting Gust, unleashes a mighty gale of wind in a targeted direction. This immense gust affects all units in its path - friend and foe alike - blasting them a great distance across the map.\n\nUse it strategically to clear defenders from a location you wish to attack, or to create a swift escape from an unfavorable battle. You can even blast your own army over impassable terrain like cliffs and rivers, repositioning them for a surprise attack.\n\n\n\nTo call down Fūjin's wind, select the Smiting Gust icon, then click and drag in the direction you wish the wind to blow."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_GODPOWER_SHOGUN"   ;   Str = "Hachiman's God Power transforms one of your Daimyo into the formidable Shogun. This powerful hero retains the traits of a Daimyo but is much stronger in combat. As the Shogun deals damage to his foes, he summons loyal Samurai directly to his side on the battlefield.\n\nThe Shogun is powerful soldier, but he cannot be healed, and his immense power will make him a prime target for your enemy.\n\nTo turn your Daimyo into a Shogun, select the Shogun icon at the top of the screen, then select your Daimyo."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_GODPOWER_THUNDER_BURST"   ;   Str = "Raijin's God Power causes violent tremors to affect the earth in the targeted area. Rings of lighting will pulse out from the epicenter, damaging and stunning enemy units.\n\nTo call down Raijin's wrath, select the Thunder Burst icon at the top of the screen, then select a location on the map to target."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_GODPOWER_SACRED_GATE"   ;   Str = "Ōkuninushi's God Power erects a great Sacred Gate on the battlefield. This un-destroyable structure serves as a conduit for divine power that can be controlled by any player holding the area.\n\nFirst, upon its construction, the gate immediately recharges your other God Powers, granting you one free cast of each. Additionally, as long as you control a gate, the Favor cost of all your God Powers is reduced. \n\n\n\nTo erect the Sacred Gate, select the God Power icon and then choose a valid location on the map."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_GODPOWER_DRAGON_TYPHOON"   ;   Str = "Watatsumi's God Power calls upon the god himself to descend from the heavens in dragon form, whipping up a deadly typhoon that tears through the battlefield. Use this power to decimate your enemies, but beware that the raging storm also harms any of your own units or buildings caught in its path.\n\nTo smite your enemies with Watatsumi's raging storm, select the Dragon Typhoon icon at the top of the screen, and then select where you want the storm to start."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_GODPOWER_DIVINE_SLASH"   ;   Str = "Takemikazuchi's God Power causes the god himself to intervene in a battle with a ferocious katana strike. Use it to cleave through enemy armies and fortifications, stunning any survivors. Beware, however, that the divine sword does not distinguish between friend and foe.\n\nTo call down Takemikazuchi's wrath, select the Divine Slash icon, then click and drag to define the path of destruction."
// End of Japanese God Power History
// Japanese God History
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_ARCHAICAGEAMATERASU"   ;   Str = "Amaterasu, the sun goddess, is the chief deity of the Japanese kami. She is the daughter of the primordial gods Izanagi and Izanami, who created the Japanese archipelago with a jeweled “heavenly spear.” Amaterasu was given Takamagahara, the homeland of the kami, to rule, while her brother Susanoo was given the underworld.\n\nA major myth surrounding Amaterasu involves her brother Susanoo. Amaterasu allowed Susanoo to live in the Takamagahara, despite the fact that he was a god of the underworld, and he began causing chaos and destruction in the world. He polluted rice fields, and even once threw a rotting horse carcass into Amaterasu’s palace. Amaterasu, in response to these actions, decided to retreat into a cave, taking the sunlight with her and leaving the world in darkness. The other kami were forced to trick her out of the cave to save the world from perpetual darkness. A kami named Ame-no-Uzume was eventually able to lure her out.\n\nAmaterasu’s main place of worship is the Grand Shrine of Ise, which is rebuilt every twenty years with lumber from a nearby sacred forest."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_ARCHAICAGETSUKUYOMI"   ;   Str = "The second-to-last child of Izanagi and Izanami, Tsukuyomi is the god of night and the moon. He is typically paired in mythology with his sister Amaterasu, the sun goddess. However, much like Amaterasu’s other brother Susanoo, Tsukuyomi often made havoc for her. Once, Amaterasu sent Tsukuyomi to meet a goddess named Ukemochi, the kami of food. After witnessing what he believed was an act of pollution, Tsukuyomi killed Ukemochi, drawing the ire of Amaterasu. Some versions of the myth state that this was when Tsukuyomi was given dominion over the moon so Amaterasu never had to set eyes on him again."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_ARCHAICAGESUSANOO"   ;   Str = "Susanoo is the god of storms, the underworld, and the tempestuous sea. He was the last child of Izanagi and Izanami and the brother of Amaterasu. After harassing Amaterasu so badly that she cast the land into perpetual darkness, the other kami banished Susanoo from the heavens, leaving him to wander the earth.\n\nWhile Susanoo lived on earth, he encountered a fearsome beast: an eight-headed black dragon (Yamata-no-Orochi). After feeding the dragon eight barrels of sake, Susanoo was able to easily kill the beast. Inside the tail of the creature, Susanoo found a legendary sword called Kusanagi. Susanoo married the woman he saved from the black dragon and reconciled with his sister Amaterasu by giving her Kusanagi."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_CLASSICALAGEAMENOUZUME"   ;   Str = "Ame-no-Uzume is perhaps most famous for luring Amaterasu out of her cave after the sun goddess had fled, taking sunlight with her and casting the land into darkness. Ame-no-Uzume, who is consequently the patron goddess of dancers, enticed Amaterasu out of the cave with a provocative dance, which made the rest of the kami laugh so hard that Amaterasu could not help but leave the cave to see what had happened.\n\nAme-no-Uzume is also considered the goddess of the arts, dawn, and sexuality. She is also known for being cunning, powerful, and literally cutthroat. One myth states that when the sea cucumber refused to serve a child of the gods, she slit its throat: to this day, the sea cucumber remains silent."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_CLASSICALAGEINARIOKAMI"   ;   Str = "Inari Ōkami is one of the most popular kami in Japan. She is the god of many things, including foxes, fertility, rice, prosperity, and sake. Inari is closely associated with foxes and is even said to appear as one at times. Her shrines are guarded by statues of these sacred creatures and are easily recognized by their deep red buildings, rows of torii gates, and Inari’s symbol – the flaming jewel. Inari’s main shrine is outside Kyōto at Fushimi Inari."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_CLASSICALAGEMINAKATATOMI"   ;   Str = "Minakatatomi is the god of hunters and warriors, as well as wind and water. His shrine is located on Lake Suwa, where, according to legend, he was once forced to beg for his life after losing a wrestling match to another kami named Takemikazuchi. In exchange for his life, Minakatatomi vowed to settle at the Suwa Shrine and relinquish his lands. He was a particular favorite of some major Japanese families to worship during the medieval period, including the Takeda and Hojō families. "
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_HEROICAGEFUJIN"   ;   Str = "As the god of wind, Fūjin is typically depicted with a bag of wind that he can open and use to control its direction. He is also commonly paired with Raijin, the thunder god. Some sources refer to them as twin brothers, while others simply consider them companions.\n\nFūjin’s appearance is particularly fearsome: he has fangs, demonic horns, and clawed hands and feet. He also has green skin, red hair, and typically wears a leopard skin to further emphasize his wildness."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_HEROICAGEHACHIMAN"   ;   Str = "Although he is typically identified as the Japanese god of war, Hachiman is also associated with protection, peace, culture, and writing. He is incredibly popular in Japan and served as the patron kami of the Minamoto warrior family.\n\nSome sources indicate that Hachiman is the deified name of the Emperor Ōjin, who was probably born in the 3rd century. Emperor Ōjin’s miraculous birth adds further intrigue to the legend of Hachiman: his mother, Empress Jingū, was on a campaign in Korea when she was pregnant with him. Afraid to give birth in the middle of a warzone, Empress Jingū carried the baby for three years and only gave birth once the campaign had been won."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_HEROICAGERAIJIN"   ;   Str = "Raijin, the god of thunder, lightning, and storms, is depicted as a demonic figure with red skin and horns. He’s commonly shown playing the drums that float around him, using them to create vicious thunderstorms. He is commonly paired with Fūjin, the wind god. Some sources refer to them as twin brothers, while others simply consider them companions.\n\nOne of the strangest legends about Raijin is his proclivity for human navels – if they are left unprotected, Raijin may try to consume them. Men in Japan would wear a stomach wrap called a haramaki to protect their navels from his attention."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_MYTHICAGEOKUNINUSHI"   ;   Str = "Ōkuninushi, whose name means “Master of the Land,” is a major earth kami in Japan. He is revered as the god of protection, agriculture, and medicine. Many of his myths highlight his compassionate nature, especially in contrast to his cruel eighty older brothers. One well-known tale is that of the flayed rabbit. In the story, his brothers advised a flayed rabbit to bathe in the salty ocean to heal its wounds. As the rabbit suffered in the saltwater, Ōkuninushi approached and suggested that it bathe in the fresh water of a nearby river instead. His kindness toward the rabbit earned him the hand of the beautiful Yakami of Inaba. Later, Ōkuninushi fell in love with another woman, Suseri, a princess and the daughter of the storm god Susanoo. The two married and eventually fled from Susanoo’s house."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_MYTHICAGEWATATSUMI"   ;   Str = "Watatsumi is the great kami of the sea and one of the sons of Izanagi and Izanami. As an island nation, the sea has always held exceptional importance for the Japanese – it served as a major food source and facilitated trade in and out of Japan. Because of this, Watatsumi is a particularly revered kami among the Japanese people. He is often depicted as an ancient aquatic dragon who dwells in a magnificent underwater palace. One of his most treasured possessions is a pair of tide-controlling gems – one that raises the tide and one that lowers it – which he bestowed upon his son-in-law, Hoori."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_MYTHICAGETAKEMIKAZUCHI"   ;   Str = "Takemikazuchi, like Raijin, is also a thunder god. In a land of constantly unpredictable weather, it is no surprise that the Japanese worshipped many of the kami associated with the fearsome storms that plagued them. Takemikazuchi is also known for subduing earthquakes, further linking him to the natural forces that frequently affect Japan. According to legend, he once defeated Namazu, a massive catfish said to swim beneath the earth and cause earthquakes.\n\nIn addition to his role as a weather deity, Takemikazuchi is a warrior god closely associated with swords. In one famous myth, he stabbed a wave with the hilt of his sword and sat atop the point. He is also connected to martial arts, and to this day, some dojos in Japan maintain shrines dedicated to him."
// End of Japanese God History
// Japanese Tech History

Several civilizations have upgrades for classes, such as infantry and cavalry. Egyptians upgrade every unit independently. The Japanese may have their line upgrades granted for free through bushidō."

ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_CONSCRIPTDOJOSOLDIERS"   ;   Str = "The last resort for a ruler needing soldiers was the forced enlistment of available able-bodied men into military service. This compulsory draft of manpower, or conscription, was disruptive and unpleasant, and a step not taken lightly. Unless conscripts were well led and highly motivated, they usually performed poorly – but conscription did put soldiers into the field quickly, and this could make the difference in an emergency."

ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_LEVYDOJOSOLDIERS"   ;   Str = "Where a population had a large percentage of non-slaves, the government usually made provision to mobilize the free men for military service. Citizens always available on standby in emergencies were called the levy. The ruler could call out the levy under certain conditions, but especially if enemies were approaching. The levy was a formal understanding and obligation, roughly equivalent to a tax or mandatory public service. Greek adult freemen, for example, were expected to serve in their town’s levy."
