ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_SACREDCUSTODIANS"   ;   Str = "Those tasked with the all-important role of maintaining Shinto shrines and performing ceremonies and rituals are known as shinshoku (male priests) or saishu (supreme priestesses). These sacred custodians of the thousands of Shinto shrines across Japan are trained by the Jinja Honcho (Association of Shinto Shrines) before being assigned to a shrine or temple."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_SAKURAGARDENS"   ;   Str = "The sakura tree, also known as the Japanese cherry blossom, is one of the most iconic symbols of Japan. These beautiful ornamental trees and their delicate pink flowers adorn parks, temples, and streets across the country – and in many places around the world. Blooming in the spring, they attract tourists from across the globe who travel to Japan to witness their beauty."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_KAGURA"   ;   Str = "Kagura is a traditional Shinto ritual dance, typically performed in dedication to various deities. It is often held at the imperial court, Shinto shrines, or sacred gardens. The origins of kagura trace back to the famous myth in which the goddess Ame-no-Uzume dances to entice the sun goddess Amaterasu out of her cave, restoring light to the world."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_GOHEIWANDS"   ;   Str = "The Gohei wand is an important artifact used in Shintō rites and rituals. Believed to possess protective powers, it is employed in purification ceremonies and offerings. A Gohei typically resembles a staff and is adorned with white cloth or paper streamers."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_KATAGI"   ;   Str = "A term that means “temperament” or “character” in Japanese cultural history, katagi was used as a moral code for various social classes in Japan. Perhaps the most important code, however, was bushi katagi, or the “warrior temperament,” which was associated with bushidō and the samurai code of conduct."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_WINDSICKLES"   ;   Str = "Inspired by the deadly Kamaitachi or ‘sickle-weasels’ of Japanese mythology, these demonic weasels blow in on gusts of wind and slice up unsuspecting humans."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_DEADLYRAGE"   ;   Str = "Oni are known for their ungovernable rage, which can lead to intense calamities. Ruled by their unhinged emotions, they can cause immense destruction with their superhuman strength when they lose control."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_GALESFURY"   ;   Str = "As the kami of winds and storms, Fūjin can unleash his bag of wind to whip up furious storms to punish his enemies."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_ONIMASK"   ;   Str = "Oni are gigantic, demonic beasts with horns that feature prominently in Japanese mythology and legend. Oni masks – physical masks worn by humans – have been attested in early Japanese folklore and are still used in modern-day Japan. People believe these masks can ward off bad luck, attract good fortune, or intimidate one’s enemies."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_ASCETICPRACTICES"   ;   Str = "Ascetic practices appear in both Shintō and Buddhist traditions. Seeking to cleanse the soul and dispel negative energies, ascetic hermits often isolate themselves in the mountains or submerge themselves in waterfalls, aiming to attain enlightenment through communion with nature. Groups such as the Yamabushi, practitioners of the syncretic tradition known as Shugendō, embody these practices by undertaking rigorous physical and spiritual training in remote, mountainous regions."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_DANNOURATACTICS"   ;   Str = "Dan-no-ura Tactics is named after one of the most famous naval battles in Japanese history. Fought between Honshū and Kyūshū Islands, the Battle of Dan-no-ura marked the culmination of a five-year war between the Taira and Minamoto clans. On April 25th, 1185 CE, the Minamoto fleet outflanked the Taira ships in a decisive sea battle, securing victory and control of Japan."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_EIGHTBANNERS"   ;   Str = "Hachiman is known as the Kami of Eight Banners (“Yahata-no-kami”) because eight banners descended from the heavens at his birth, symbolizing his divine right to rule as emperor."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_GOLDENKITE"   ;   Str = "In Japanese mythology, the golden kite bird served as a messenger of the kami. According to legend, Emperor Jimmu was aided by a golden kite during his conquest of Japan. The bird holds a lasting place in Japan’s cultural memory, which may explain its inclusion in a prestigious military decoration: the Order of the Golden Kite."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_DEADLYSNARE"   ;   Str = "The Jorogumo can use her webs to ensnare her victims, usually young men whom she wishes to drag to their deaths. The Jorogumo is also associated with the venomous Joro spider, which can be found throughout East Asia."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_IVORYNETSUKE"   ;   Str = "Netsukes are intricately carved small sculptures that were first created during the Edo period. Traditionally carved from ivory, these miniature works often depicted scenes from folklore and legends rooted in Japanese tradition. From an economic standpoint, netsukes were incredibly popular both within Japan and in foreign markets – especially in Europe."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_SASHIMONOBANNERMEN"   ;   Str = "Sashimono banners were worn by both samurai and foot soldiers during Japan's feudal period. Each sashimono consisted of a rectangular banner attached to an L-shaped wooden frame, bearing various identifying symbols or crests used to indicate which feudal family the warriors served."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_WISDOMOFNINE"   ;   Str = "The nine-tailed Kitsune is closely associated with the god Inari Ōkami, as both are symbols of prosperity. The fox's nine tails are believed to signify not only good fortune and wisdom but also a promise of many descendants."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_CONDEMNEDSOUL"   ;   Str = "As servants of hell, the primary role of the Wanyūdō yōkai is to drag condemned souls down to the underworld, where they are judged for their crimes. The Wanyūdō itself is also a condemned soul – typically someone who was evil in life and transformed into a demonic servant of hell."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_HUNTERSSTRENGTH"   ;   Str = "Minakatatomi was known as the god of hunting and warfare. While archery was more commonly associated with warfare, hunters still occasionally used the bow and arrow to hunt deer and boar."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_KUMIKI"   ;   Str = "Kumiki are traditional Japanese wooden puzzles inspired by the carpentry technique of the same name. Kumiki carpentry joins wooden components without the use of nails, glue, or other fasteners. This method was primarily used in the construction of wooden shrines across Japan and also made buildings more resilient to earthquakes."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_SALTWATERSPRING"   ;   Str = "Onsen is the Japanese word for hot springs. In both early and modern Japanese traditions, hot springs are regarded as spaces of healing and wellness. Suijin, the kami of springs and water, is often worshipped at these sites. To honor Suijin, it is customary to place flowers and sake near the spring before using it."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_DIVINEPREFECTURE"   ;   Str = "As a god of the earth, it follows that Ōkuninushi is associated with sacred spaces on land. In Japan, one of the most revered prefectures is Mie Prefecture, home to the Ise Grand Shrine."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_MECHANICALARTISANS"   ;   Str = "The oyumi is a technical marvel – a large siege crossbow that first came into use in Japan during the 7th century CE. Difficult to operate, the oyumi could be handled only by the strongest and most skilled soldiers."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_RESTLESSARMY"   ;   Str = "The Onmoraki is a frightful beast, and some legends claim it originates from the restless spirits of the recently deceased. If a person dies without receiving the proper burial rites, their spirit may transform into an Onmoraki, eventually contributing to an army of restless souls."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_DENDENDRUMS"   ;   Str = "A major aspect of Raijin’s control over storms and thunder comes from his magical drums, which are often depicted encircling his upper torso in Japanese artwork. The den-den daiko – a double-sided spinner drum – is perhaps one of the most iconic drums in Shinto culture and is still commonly used in religious ceremonies today."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_HANNYAMASK"   ;   Str = "The Hannya mask is traditionally used in Japanese Noh theater to portray female demon figures. These masks are crafted to express intense emotions – most commonly jealousy and anger. Hannya masks are also used in certain Shinto rituals, such as kagura dances."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_HEAVENLYBARRAGE"   ;   Str = "Raijin, the god of thunder, lightning, and storms, is depicted as a demonic figure with red skin and horns. He’s commonly shown playing the drums that float around him, using them to create vicious thunderstorms and hurl lightning bolts from the sky."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_THUNDEROUSPRESENCE"   ;   Str = "A Japanese expression states, “There are four frightening things: earthquakes, fire, thunder, and father.” As an island nation frequently plagued by natural disasters, especially storms, it is no surprise that Japanese mythology includes multiple thunder kami. Two of the most well-known are Raijin and Takemikazuchi."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_CRUSHINGWAVES"   ;   Str = "Susanoo, known for his wild moods and chaotic nature, is reflected in the domain he rules: the sea. Like a crashing tidal wave, he can destroy and punish; like a calm sea, he can be benevolent and humble."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_BURNINGMALEVOLENCE"   ;   Str = "Takemikazuchi is sometimes known as an aramitama, or a ‘rough spirit’. Kami that are called aramitama are known for their wild or uncontrollable emotions, such as rage, jealously, or violence."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_SOJUTSU"   ;   Str = "Sōjutsu, or the art of the spear, has been practiced in Japan for centuries. It originated with the samurai, who trained in sōjutsu to master the yari, a traditional Japanese spear used during the feudal era. Today, the discipline continues to be practiced by thousands, serving as a living expression of cultural memory and martial heritage."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_SUMOTRAINING"   ;   Str = "The history of sumo wrestling in Japan dates back to the Kofun period and is mentioned in both the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki. Sumo became professionalized during the Edo period, when many feudal warlords became avid supporters of the sport. Among them was Oda Nobunaga, who held large tournaments annually."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_TENFISTSWORD"   ;   Str = "The Ten-Fist Sword, or Totsuka-no-Tsurugi, is a legendary weapon used by various kami in Japanese mythology, including Izanagi, Amaterasu, and Susanoo. The name “Ten-Fist” refers to the sword’s length, roughly equivalent to ten hand spans."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_TENSHU"   ;   Str = "The tenshu is a distinctive architectural feature of Japanese castles. First appearing in the 16th century, it typically serves as the tallest tower within the castle complex, featuring multiple stories, sloping roofs, and ornate decorations."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_ASSYMETRICALBOWS"   ;   Str = "One of the most unique weapons in Japan is the yumi: an asymmetrical Japanese longbow that was typically seven to nine feet in length and could be used both on foot and on horseback. The asymmetrical design involves an off-centered grip, closer to the lower half of the bow."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_ETERNALHAUNTING"   ;   Str = "The death spirits known as Shinigami are notorious for haunting wicked souls and forcing them to commit atrocious acts. What is less well known is the idea that Shinigami can eternally haunt a certain area of Japan, affecting not only their initial victim, but the community as a whole."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_ONMYODO"   ;   Str = "Onmyōdō, sometimes translated as the “Way of Yin and Yang,” is a discipline that encompasses the study of astrology, divination, and calendar systems."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_SEASIDEINFILTRATORS"   ;   Str = "As the god who is master of the tides, Watatsumi’s control of the seaside allows shinobi to sneak around the coastline to spy on their enemies."
// End of Japanese Tech History
// Japanese Unit History
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_CARAVANJAPANESE"   ;   Str = "As Japan was a small, insular island nation, it relied on trade with other countries to access a wider variety of resources. One particularly notable period of trade in feudal Japan was the Nanban trade period, which began with the arrival of Portuguese traders in 1543 CE. Through these connections, Japan became linked to trade networks involving many diverse European countries. The Portuguese also helped facilitate trade between Japan and various East Asian countries – the most prominent of these being China. Japan primarily exported silver and copper, while importing highly coveted Chinese silks."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_VILLAGERJAPANESE"   ;   Str = "The commoner class in feudal Japan comprised roughly 90% of the population. At the top of this class were farmers and fishermen, as they provided food for the rest of the country. Next came the artisans – craftspeople who created tools, weapons, and everyday items essential to both the upper and lower classes. Finally, the lowest tier was occupied by merchants and traders. Although markets were integral to society, the Japanese viewed traders as inferior because they did not produce anything themselves."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_FISHINGSHIPJAPANESE"   ;   Str = "Fishing has always been an important source of sustenance in East Asia, spurring a constant drive to improve technologies related to fishing boats, nets, navigation, and traps. Evidence of these fishing technologies has been unearthed at sites dating back six thousand years.\n\nAs an island nation, the Japanese have had a long and rich tradition of fishing. With more than 18,480 miles of coastline, they have always had access to vast varieties of seafood. Men fished offshore and developed sophisticated techniques for catching large fish such as tuna and dolphins, while women collected shellfish on the shoreline. A special class of women called Ama, or sea women, were trained to hold their breath for long periods so they could dive for shellfish and pearls."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_BUSHI"   ;   Str = "The Japanese term “bushi” translates to “warrior” and is the most appropriate term when referring to a professional Japanese warrior. The term was initially used in the Nara Era (710-784 CE) and predates the more culturally popular concept of the Japanese warrior, the samurai, by a few centuries. Bushi soldiers are associated with “Bushidō” or “The Way of the Warrior,” a code of conduct for professional warriors in Japan. Bushidō dictates eight virtues that the bushi were expected to follow: justice, courage, mercy, politeness, honesty, honor, loyalty, and character."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_DAIMYO"   ;   Str = "Daimyos were powerful feudal lords who led bands of professional warriors. They were a level above the bushi and were at their most powerful from 1185-1868 CE. They not only controlled warriors but also served as powerful landholders over vast estates.\n\nThree of the most famous historical daimyōs are Oda Nobunaga (1534-1582 CE), Toyotomi Hideyoshi (1537-1598 CE), and Tokugawa Ieyasu (1543-1616 CE), who history remembers as the three unifiers of Japan."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_ONMYOJI"   ;   Str = "An Onmyōji is a practitioner of Onmyōdō, a form of magic associated with fortune-telling and divination. They were employed by the noble class to provide advice and guidance on matters such as battle plans, marriage prospects, political decisions, and more. Beyond their divination skills, Onmyōji were repositories of knowledge in areas such as Japanese folklore, astrological calendars, the philosophy of yin and yang, and the five elements.\n\nOnmyōji were highly valued in the various courts of the shoguns and held considerable influence during the feudal period. Perhaps the most famous Onmyōji was Abe-no-Seimei (921–1005 CE), who lived during the Heian period."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_ONNAMUSHA"   ;   Str = "The onna-musha were an elite class of warrior women in feudal Japan. They were skilled in archery, horseback riding, and martial arts. One of the most famous onna-musha in history was Tomoe Gozen, who was born in 1157 CE and, according to some rumors, lived to be ninety years old. Stories tell of her fearlessness, ability to lead others on the battlefield, and her significant role in the Genpei War. In one particularly famous battle, when her leader Yoshinaka was mortally wounded, Tomoe rode out and beheaded Onda no Hachiro Morishige before escaping the battlefield."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_SHOGUN"   ;   Str = "The shoguns were elite military rulers in feudal Japan. The term, which means 'commander of the army,' was first applied at the end of the Heian period. The first shogunate was formed by Minamoto no Yoritomo in 1192 CE, establishing the position of shogun as a hereditary military dictatorship until 1867 CE. The shoguns were only one step below the emperor, making them among the most powerful individuals in Japan. However, the shoguns eventually gained so much power that the emperor became merely a symbolic figurehead, while the true power rested with the shoguns."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_JUNKOZOSEN"   ;   Str = "Junkozosen were rudimentary, semi-structured boats used in ancient Japan. A step above dugout canoes, Junkozosen had added side planks to improve stability in ocean waters. Scholars agree that the transition from dugout canoes to Junkozosen was a direct response to the rise in maritime trade – as inter-regional trade became more frequent in medieval Japan, the need for advances in shipbuilding grew. In addition to trade, Junkozosen were also used for fishing. They were typically constructed from cedar or cypress trees. This semi-structured boat represents a transitional period between simple canoes and fully structured plank ships."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_MIKO"   ;   Str = "A miko is a female shaman in the Shinto religion. In earlier periods, mikos were believed to serve as vessels through which the kami could communicate – the spirit of a kami could possess the miko and deliver oracles or prophecies through her. Miko either served as shamans at shrines, conducting sacred rites, or traveled from shrine to shrine, demonstrating their magical and religious powers through fortune-telling. In modern times, mikos typically work at Shinto shrines, where they perform sacred cleansings and a ritual dance known as kagura."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_NAGINATARIDER"   ;   Str = "Wielding the fearsome and iconic naginata, this rider pays tribute to one of Japan’s most distinctive weapons, highly valued by the samurai class. Sometimes mistakenly referred to as a halberd, the naginata features a wickedly sharp, three-foot-long curved blade affixed to a wooden shaft. Because it could be used for both cutting and thrusting – and offered the warrior a wide range of attack – the naginata was favored by both cavalry and infantry. It became so effective in combat that some scholars believe Japanese armor evolved in response, incorporating stronger protection for the lower body and legs to defend against its sweeping strikes."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_OYUMI"   ;   Str = "The oyumi is a large siege crossbow that first came into use in Japan during the 7th century. Initially fixed to a platform and unable to rotate, it was upgraded in the 9th century to allow greater mobility and the ability to fire multiple arrows simultaneously. Though deadly, the oyumi was difficult to operate – only the strongest and most skilled soldiers could handle this formidable weapon. Historical evidence suggests that these siege engines were used well into the 12th century."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_RAMMINGWASEN"   ;   Str = "The wasen is a traditional Japanese wooden plank ship. Japanese daimyō often used these ships for military purposes: whether sailing around the Japanese archipelago or attacking neighboring countries, they would transport samurai warriors to neighboring countries to try and conquer them. For example, in the 16th century, Toyotomi Hideyoshi invaded Korea with 9,000 ships, and in 1609 CE, Shimazu Tadatsune invaded the Ryūkyū Islands with 2,500 samurai. Although their neighbors – such as the Chinese and the Koreans – historically had stronger navies, Japanese soldiers were exceptionally skilled in close combat, particularly during shipboard engagements."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_SAMURAI"   ;   Str = "Perhaps the most iconic military figures in Japanese history, the samurai were a powerful warrior class in feudal Japan. Governed by a strict code of honor – bushidō, or “the way of the warrior” – these elite fighters aspired to live disciplined, moral lives. The samurai first emerged during the Heian period but rose to prominence in the 12th century, as political power shifted away from the emperor and toward powerful regional clans.\n\nAmong the many legendary samurai, the most famous is likely Miyamoto Musashi, who lived during the late 16th and early 17th centuries. Renowned for his unmatched swordsmanship, Musashi famously dueled his rival Sasaki Kojirō in 1612, defeating him with a wooden sword he had made from an oar."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_SHINOBI"   ;   Str = "Shinobi no mono, or “those who sneak,” were Japanese spies who emerged during the Sengoku Period. More commonly known as ninja in popular culture, shinobi were masters of concealment and deception. They would disguise themselves to gather intelligence, taking on the appearance of anything from a common villager to a komusō (a wandering flute-playing monk), or even something as unexpected as a pile of firewood.\n\nAlthough espionage was typically their primary role, shinobi also served as ruthless assassins. Operating under the cover of night and clad in dark clothing, they were tasked with eliminating the political enemies of those they served. In combat, they often wielded a katana, the iconic single-edged, two-handed sword, but are also famed for improvised weapons and projectiles such as the kusarigama and shuriken."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_TRANSPORTSHIPJAPANESE"   ;   Str = "Historically, Japanese ships were used to transport soldiers, citizens, traders, and foreign diplomats both within the country and throughout Southeast Asia. However, during certain historical periods – such as the national seclusion policy in the 17th century – some forms of international travel were banned. Because of this, the shogunate restricted the size of ships being built, limiting them to a maximum of 49 gross tons. There were many different types of transport ships, including the higaki kaisen, the bezaisen, and the hirata-bune."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_WASEN"   ;   Str = "The wasen is a traditional Japanese wooden plank ship. Japanese daimyō often used these ships for military purposes: whether sailing around the Japanese archipelago or attacking neighboring countries, they would transport samurai warriors to neighboring countries to try and conquer them. For example, in the 16th century, Toyotomi Hideyoshi invaded Korea with 9,000 ships, and in 1609 CE, Shimazu Tadatsune invaded the Ryūkyū Islands with 2,500 samurai. Although their neighbors – such as the Chinese and the Koreans – historically had stronger navies, Japanese soldiers were exceptionally skilled in close combat, particularly during shipboard engagements."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_WOLFSHRINEOFTHEHUNT"   ;   Str = "Wolves appear frequently in Japanese legends. One of the most famous stories involves the folktale of the senbiki ōkami – a mythical pack of wolves that roam mountain roads at night. If a traveler is unfortunate enough to be walking alone and the wolves encounter them, the traveler typically escapes by climbing up a tree. What makes the ōkami wolves especially supernatural is that, once their human prey is treed, they climb on top of one another to form a living ladder in an attempt to reach the unlucky traveler."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_YARISPEARMAN"   ;   Str = "The yari is a distinctive Japanese spear primarily used by infantry soldiers, though it was occasionally carried by cavalry as well. Its length varied depending on the individual clan, each of which had specific preferences – some yari measured over eight feet long. The spearhead was typically made of steel and featured a triangular cross-section near the base with a long, sharp point.\n\nThe yari was never thrown in battle; it was used exclusively for hand-to-hand combat. A few legendary warriors are remembered for their exceptional skill with the yari, including Watanabe Hanzō, known as “Hanzō of the Yari,” and Fukube Koheita, who killed Imagawa Yoshimoto with a yari in 1560 CE."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_YUMIARCHER"   ;   Str = "The yumi, a Japanese longbow, was typically seven to nine feet in length and could be used both on foot and on horseback. What makes the yumi particularly distinctive is its asymmetrical design – the grip is positioned off-center, closer to the lower half of the bow. Constructed with a wooden core and strips of bamboo laminated together, this unique design made the yumi an exceptionally powerful and deadly weapon for samurai, onna-musha, and other warriors to wield in battle. In Japan, the art of archery was known as Kyūdō, and it was regarded with great seriousness and discipline.\n\nThe term yumi also has mythological significance. One of the famous legendary weapons in Japanese mythology is Ikuyumiya, or “the life bow-and-arrows.” It originally belonged to the god Susanoo, but was stolen by his son-in-law, Ōkuninushi, who later returned the legendary weapon."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_YUMIHORSEARCHER"   ;   Str = "The yumi, a Japanese longbow, was typically seven to nine feet in length and could be used both on foot and on horseback. What makes the yumi particularly distinctive is its asymmetrical design – the grip is positioned off-center, closer to the lower half of the bow. Constructed with a wooden core and strips of bamboo laminated together, this unique design made the yumi an exceptionally powerful and deadly weapon for samurai, onna-musha, and other warriors to wield in battle. Horse archers using the yumi enjoyed an uncanny combination of immense firepower, agility, and mobility. In Japan, the art of archery was known as Kyūdō, and it was regarded with great seriousness and discipline.\n\nThe term yumi also has mythological significance. One of the famous legendary weapons in Japanese mythology is Ikuyumiya, or “the life bow-and-arrows.” It originally belonged to the god Susanoo, but was stolen by his son-in-law, Ōkuninushi, who later returned the legendary weapon."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_ASURA"   ;   Str = "Scientific name – Semideus rubrovultus ignijactor\nSize – 300 lbs\nDiet – charred meat from ritiual offerings\n\nThe Asura is a three-faced, red skinned warrior demigod that originates from Buddhist traditions but was later incorporated into the Japanese mythological world through religious syncretism. Along with having three faces, the Asura also possesses six arms, each wielding six different weapons.\n\nAsuras are known to have chaotic emotions, and they are typically driven by their base instincts: pride, violence, and greed. As beings of chaos and war, it is fitting that they are associated with fire – they hurl fireballs with several of their many arms to incinerate their enemies when provoked."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_HONENGYO"   ;   Str = "Scientific name – Monstrum fluminis faustum\nSize – 165 lbs\nDiet – small river fish and seaweed\n\nThe Honengyo is an amphibious, water-dwelling yōkai that lives in the rivers of Japan. Although this beast has a fearsome appearance – measuring over two meters in length, with dark scales, razor-sharp black dorsal fins and mirror-like eyes – the Honengyo is typically interpreted as a good sign for the Japanese people, especially for fisherman. When a Honengyo washes up on shore, it serves as an omen for a prosperous fishing season.\n\nOne of the most famous Honengyo sightings occurred in 1866 CE, when one washed up the shore of the Yodo River. The artistic depictions of the creature that were drawn after this sighting are sometimes rumored to be the inspiration for the appearance of the pop culture monster Godzilla."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_JOROGUMO"   ;   Str = "Scientific name – Maga aranea\nSize – 110 lbs\nDiet –  human flesh\n\nThe Jorogumo is a yōkai that is a spider-human hybrid: sometimes she appears as a beautiful woman, and sometimes she can transform into a spider. Jorogumos can command webs and smaller spiders to do their bidding. They typically appear in Japanese mythology as temptress figures: one such example is the Jorogumo who was mistress of the Jōren Falls of Izu. Legends about her tell of a beautiful woman that would try to ensnare men with webs at Jōren Falls and drag them to their doom."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_KAMAITACHI"   ;   Str = "Scientific name – Mustela venti cultellifera\nSize – 10 lbs\nDiet – small animals\n\nKnown as the “sickle-weasel” in Japanese mythology, the Kamaitachi is a vicious Mustelidae creature with knife-like claws and spiny fur. Perhaps most fascinating is that Kamaitachi attack in groups of three: they ride the wind and strike humans from the air.\n\nAlthough the three Kamaitachi cause serious damage in these whirlwind assaults, they simultaneously heal some of their victims’ wounds. A Kamaitachi attack is never fatal, despite their ability to inflict precise injuries with their deadly blades."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_KITSUNE"   ;   Str = "Scientific name – Vulpes dolosa\nSize – 12 lbs\nDiet – small mammals and insects\n\nThe Kitsune, which literally means “fox” in Japanese, is a yōkai that serves as a trickster figure in Japanese mythology. While Kitsunes have many mythical powers, one of their most impressive is transfiguration: there are legends of Kitsunes transforming into beautiful women to seduce men. These so-called “fox-wives” were not necessarily dangerous, but sometimes humans would fall victim to fox possession, a very serious condition. Affected individuals might bark like dogs, eat off the floor, and develop an intense fear of dogs. This often led to social ostracism, demonstrating that Kitsunes could be harmful tricksters.\n\nHowever, not all Kitsunes are mischievous. Some are considered benevolent and are associated with the god Inari Ōkami, serving as guardians of her shrines. They also act as Inari Ōkami's messengers, and sightings of these Kitsunes are believed to symbolize good luck and a bountiful harvest. Kitsunes are also associated with long life – they are said to live for thousands of years."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_ONI"   ;   Str = "Scientific name – Rubrum monstrum\nSize – 800 lbs\nDiet – human flesh and sake\n\nOnis are gigantic, demonic, ogre-like beasts with red skin and horns. They are typically depicted wearing tiger-print loincloths, symbolizing their wild nature. In their clawed hands, Onis often wield a tetsubō – a heavy, spiked club – which they use to batter and punish sinners sent to hell.\n\nOnis are not confined to hell; they are also believed to inhabit the earth. However, they can be banished from earth on the second day of the second month during Setsubun, a traditional Japanese festival. This day features a ritual called mamemaki, in which people throw beans to ward off Onis and bad luck."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_ONMORAKI"   ;   Str = "Scientific name – Avis spiritus terribilis\nSize – 33 lbs\nDiet – fear and regret from humans\n\nThe Onmoraki is a grim, monstrous winged creature with a humanoid face and a sharp beak. With a haunting, shrill voice, the Onmoraki is known to plague neglectful religious officials outside of shrines and temples. One of its most chilling talents is the ability to imitate the voice of the person that it is harassing, taunting them about their failures.\n\nSome Japanese legends suggest that Onmorakis originate from recently deceased people with unsettled spirits – if a human dies without the correct burial practice, their spirit transforms into an Onmoraki."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_RAIJU"   ;   Str = "Scientific name – Lupus fulminis\nSize – 180 lbs\nDiet – Lightning bolts\n\nThe Raiju is a yōkai associated with thunderstorms and lightning – its name translates to “thunder beast.” This wolf-like creature, with long and deadly claws, is said to leave lightning burns on trees with its paws. Raijus are also believed to summon lightning, and some myths even claim they ride lightning bolts. Closely linked to Raijin, the Japanese god of thunder, the Raiju features in various legends. Some myths state that Raijus like to curl up inside human navels, which leads some superstitious people to protect their navels during storms."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_SHINIGAMI"   ;   Str = "Scientific name – Spiritus mortis pallidus\nSize – 160 lbs\nDiet – death and despair\n\nA haunting spirit of the dead, the Shinigami is a vengeful ghost with gray hair and a translucent appearance. Although Shinigamis appear human-like, their sunken, sallow skin and eerie features make it clear that these beings are demons from the afterlife. These death spirits haunt areas where people have recently died. Local legends across Japan suggest that acts of violence invite more violence – explaining the appearance of the Shinigami. Some stories claim that the Shinigami can possess people and drive them to commit violent acts."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_TENGU"   ;   Str = "Scientific name – Viravis ruber\nSize – 150 lbs\nDiet – fruit, grains, and sake\n\nThe Tengu is a bird-like humanoid creature with red skin and wings. These beings dwell deep in the mountains of Japan and have long been associated with peculiar occurrences, such as unexpected falling rocks and mysterious laughter echoing in the distance. Tengus sometimes deliberately confuse travelers, causing them to lose their way.\n\nHowever, if a Tengu deems a human worthy, they may impart valuable skills, the most notable being swordsmanship. Tengu are renowned swordsmen, and many myths tell of them training legendary figures in Japanese folklore. They are also proficient in magic and, on occasion, may share their mystical knowledge with humans."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_TITANYAMATANOOROCHI"   ;   Str = "Scientific name – Draco octocephalus caeruleus\nSize –500,000 lbs\nDiet – human beings\n\nYamata-no-Orochi is one of the most physically imposing figures in Japanese mythology: its massive blue-black body is said to span over eight valleys and eight hills. Furthermore, it has blood-red eyes, eight heads, and eight tails.\n\nThe Yamata-no-Orochi once terrorized a family with eight daughters: every year, the great beast would come and devour one of the girls. The kami Susanoo was wandering the earth and came upon the family before their final daughter was set to be eaten by the Yamata-no-Orochi. Susanoo was able to trick the beast into drinking eight barrels of sake, until it became so intoxicated that it fell asleep. Susanoo then killed the beast, and in one of the tails of the great dragon, he found a sword named Kusanagi."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_UMIBOZU"   ;   Str = "Scientific name – Magnaniger maris globus\nSize – Immeasurable\nDiet – unlucky sailors\n\nA mysterious aquatic giant, the Umibōzu is a calamitous presence that haunts the seas and attacks ships. Most Umibōzu sightings begin on a calm night – then, out of nowhere, the waves begin to crash and the wind starts to blow. To the horror of Japanese sailors, the Umibōzu rises from the sea; however, due to its enormous size, they have only ever seen its head.\n\nThe Umibōzu will then sink the ship unless the sailors offer it a bottomless barrel. The creature tries to fill the barrel and use it to sink the ship but cannot understand why the barrel never fills. Amidst the Umibōzu’s confusion, the sailors have a chance to escape.\n\nAccording to legend, the Umibōzu is the vengeful spirit of a drowned monk, now haunting the same villagers who once sacrificed monks to the sea."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_WANYUDO"   ;   Str = "Scientific name – Ignivolvens daemonium\nSize – 600 lbs\nDiet – the legs of small children\n\nThe Wanyūdō is a hellish, unnatural being: it appears as a giant, flaming wheel with a man's head in the center, the spokes of the wheel piercing through his skull. As a servant of hell, the Wanyūdō roams Japan in search of wicked souls to drag back to the underworld, where they can atone for their sins.\n\nTypically, a Wanyūdō is seen rolling through town along the road – villagers flee in terror, as simply being noticed by the creature can bring a terrible fate, regardless of one's innocence or guilt."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_WRETCH"   ;   Str = "Scientific Name – Telum cadavericum\nSize – 115 lbs\nDiet – nothing\n\nA wretch is a corpse that the Onmoraki uses as a projectile to hurl at enemies. Similar to the Western concept of a zombie, the wretch is a dead body that can be reanimated for a short period of time to fight for an army."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_CASTLE"   ;   Str = "Castles in Japan were traditionally built for daimyōs, who were feudal lords and part of the warrior class in feudal Japan. Castles were first built in the latter half of the 7th century, and started as rather rudimentary wooden fortresses; however, as the daimyō class gained power and absorbed weaker rivals, the fortifications became more complex, eventually incorporating stone. Then, these fortresses began to incorporate more ‘castle-like’ elements, such as gatehouses, towers, and keeps.\n\nCastles were commonly built in the mountains, relying on the natural defenses provided by the landscape. If there were no suitable mountains available in the region, artificial mounds would be created to build the castle on top of; this explains why many surviving Japanese castles have steep stone walls.\n\nOne of the most famous castles in Japan is Himeji Castle, also known as the White Heron Castle, which dates back to the 14th century."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_DOJO"   ;   Str = "The Japanese dojo is a space of spiritual reflection. Whether used for military training or meditation, it is regarded in Japanese culture as a place to “polish the soul.” In modern-day Japan, dojos are most commonly associated with martial arts and serve as spaces to practice disciplines such as judo and aikido.\n\nDojos are traditionally linked to the kami Takemikazuchi, who is revered as the patron kami of martial arts. Many dojos – especially aikido dojos – feature small shrines dedicated to Takemikazuchi, where students and masters can offer worship."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_GOSHINBOKU"   ;   Str = "Goshinboku translates to “sacred divine tree” in Japanese and refers to special, sacred trees where kami are believed to reside. They are typically found within or near Shinto shrines. One of the most popular trees associated with housing kami is the Hinoki cypress. A goshinboku can be identified by a distinctive sacred rope tied around its trunk, known as a shimenawa. These trees are highly protected in Japan, and it is strictly forbidden to cut them down or cause them harm."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_GUARDHOUSE"   ;   Str = "Guardhouses were placed outside castles to monitor anyone entering or leaving the grounds. The guardhouse was not a space for highly trained elite samurai to hang about; rather, it was typically manned by non-elite foot soldiers.\n\nOnly three guardhouses from the Edo period remain today. One of them, Doshin Bansho, is located outside the castle of the Tokugawa Shogunate, right next to the Ōte-mon Gate, which was controlled by the soldiers that were stationed in the guardhouse."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_MININGCAMPJAPANESE"   ;   Str = "As a small island nation, Japan today has few remaining natural resources. However, during the Edo period, the country was rich in valuable ore deposits. Dubbed “The Land of Gold” by Marco Polo, Japan had significant gold ore deposits on Sado Island and in the Tōhoku region. Silver was also a vital resource – notably from the Iwami Ginzan Silver Mine, which was established in the 16th century and is now a UNESCO World Heritage Site that tourists can visit today. Furthermore, at one point during the Edo period, Japan was the world’s leading producer of copper."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_SACREDGATE"   ;   Str = "The torii gate is perhaps one of the most recognizable architectural features of Japan. Typically painted vermillion, torii gates mark the entrance to sacred Shintō shrines.\n\nThough there are many variations, a torii gate generally consists of two wooden pillars with two crossbeams spanning the top. The top crossbeam is usually slightly curved. Some of the most famous torii gates in Japan can be found at the Itsukushima Shrine, Fushimi Inari Taisha, and the Hakone Shrine. There are approximately 90,000 torii gates in Japan."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_SHRINEJAPANESE"   ;   Str = "Japanese shrines are deeply associated with the Shinto religion. With an estimated 80,000 official shrines in Japan, these spaces are integral to Japanese history and tourism. Shrines are typically dedicated to an individual kami and are found in places where people felt a close connection to the kami. As a result, shrines are often located in beautiful natural settings such as forests, bodies of water, waterfalls, or mountains.\n\nShrines are marked by torii gates at the entrance and typically feature a honden (main hall) and a haiden (worship hall). Their size can vary: some miniature shrines can be found by the roadside, while others, like the Ise Grand Shrine, are large and attract millions of visitors each year.\n\nOne of the most distinctive aspects of Japanese shrines is the tradition of dismantling and rebuilding them approximately every twenty years, a practice known as Shikinen Sengu. This ritual originated in the 7th century CE and has continued for over a thousand years."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_SHRINEOFTHEHUNT"   ;   Str = "Japanese shrines are deeply associated with the Shinto religion. With an estimated 80,000 official shrines in Japan, these spaces are integral to Japanese history and tourism. Shrines are typically dedicated to an individual kami and are found in places where people felt a close connection to the kami. As a result, shrines are often located in beautiful natural settings such as forests, bodies of water, waterfalls, or mountains.\n\nShrines are marked by torii gates at the entrance and typically feature a honden (main hall) and a haiden (worship hall). Their size can vary: some miniature shrines can be found by the roadside, while others, like the Ise Grand Shrine, are large and attract millions of visitors each year.\n\nOne of the most distinctive aspects of Japanese shrines is the tradition of dismantling and rebuilding them approximately every twenty years, a practice known as Shikinen Sengu. This ritual originated in the 7th century CE and has continued for over a thousand years."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_STABLEJAPANESE"   ;   Str = "Horses have a long history on the island of Japan. Archaeological evidence suggests that they first arrived around 5,000 BCE; however, these early horses were quite small and not used for riding. It wasn’t until the early medieval period – around the 4th century CE – that horses began to be used for transportation and warfare.\n\nIn samurai culture, horses were incredibly important. Beyond serving as a status symbol, samurai frequently engaged in mounted cavalry combat. Horses also held religious significance – white horses, in particular, were revered. Some Shinto shrines even have stables on their grounds to house these sacred horses, known as shinme. These white horses are believed to transport kami (spirits or deities) between realms."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_WATERMILL"   ;   Str = "The watermill first appeared in Japan in 610 CE. Watermills have long held economic significance in Japan, playing an integral role in industries such as rice and grain milling, as well as papermaking. Water wheels became particularly important during the Edo period. One especially famous example from this time is the Asakura water wheels, which have been operating for over 230 years and still provide water to the surrounding rice fields."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_SHINOBIWANKENOBI"   ;   Str = "Common name -- The Old Wanderer\nOrder -- Master of a forgotten creed\nDiet -- Rice, green tea, cosmic justice\n\nThis warrior is the last known practitioner of a creed that balances diplomacy and aggression. Adherents to the creed were known for their elegant, energized blades and their uncanny ability to persuade the weak-minded. They are most comfortable when fighting from an elevated position, displaying tactical superiority even on perfectly level terrain. It is said their connection to a universal 'Ki' field makes them formidable opponents."
// End of Japanese Unit History

An onmyōji of the 10th century, he became a legendary figure in both history and myth after his death.\n\nKnown for his wisdom and tranquil demeanor, Seimei possesses secret knowledge and a deep connection to the gods. When he meets Yasuko, he gladly aids her on her quest, sensing that she is both sincere and destined for greatness."

ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_HARUMOTO"   ;   Str = "Age -- 35\nHomeland -- Izumi Village\nOccupation -- daimyō\n\nHarumoto, the daimyō of Yasuko and Tatsuro’s home village, is an honorable man whose foremost concern is the protection of his people. A natural-born leader, he is beloved by those whom he governs. When danger arises, Harumoto enlists Yasuko’s help and supports her throughout her quest. ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_HEAVENLYJEWELEDSPEAR"   ;   Str = "Ame-no-Nuboko, or the Heavenly Jeweled Spear of the Kami, was given to Izanami and Izanagi for the express purpose of creating land on Earth. Standing upon the Ame-no-Ukihashi, the Heavenly Floating Bridge, the two kami stirred the ocean with the spear, forming the first landmasses through the magic imbued within it.\n\nLater entrusted to Amaterasu, the Heavenly Jeweled Spear was housed in the Ise Grand Shrine – until it was stolen. It next resurfaces when a wounded warrior passes it on to a young farmer’s daughter named Yasuko, who is able to tap into the magical properties of the spear to defend those she cares about."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_KAGEMASA"   ;   Str = "Age -- 46\nHomeland -- Kyūshū\nOccupation -- conqueror\n\nKagemasa, the corrupt conqueror, was once a beloved and respected ruler. Wielding a long and deadly katana, Kagemasa seeks to conquer all of Japan – and dispose of anyone who stands in his way."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_KAPPA"   ;   Str = "Scientific name – Daemon aquarius\nSize – 85 lbs\nDiet – horse meat and cucumbers\n\nThe Kappa, a water sprite, is a particularly infamous yōkai. With the head of a monkey and a tortoise shell on its back, this violent and demonic creature is known for luring unsuspecting people into bodies of water to drown them. It often targets women and children but is also known to harm or kill horses. However, not everything about the Kappa is negative. It is an expert in setting bones, and some doctors have even sought its help to learn these medical practices. Despite its violent tendencies, the Kappa is relatively easy to outwit. Humans can bribe it with a cucumber or trick it into bowing. Atop the Kappa's head is an indentation filled with water – if this water spills when the Kappa bows, it becomes powerless and can no longer attack."

A spirit of nature, she answers to no one and follows only her own mysterious laws. She chooses to ally herself with the corrupt warlord Kagemasa, though her motives are entirely selfish."

As a mischievous youth at a Shinto temple, he once donned an Oni mask to harass the priests – only to find it had fused to his face. He once mentored a young warrior named Ushiwakamaru, and when Yasuko seeks his aid, he agrees to help. Proud and fiercely independent, Sōjōbō supports her cause, though his ultimate desire is to return to his mountain domain."

The bounty of agriculture thus needed protection almost immediately. The earliest and most simple defensive structure was the wall. The earliest towns so far discovered are walled for defense. Strong walls prevented easy movement into a town and provided cover if enemies attacked. Walls were made with whatever materials were handy and useful. Wall building has continued for thousands of years, and is still carried on today."

// Japanese Nature History
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_TREECHERRY"   ;   Str = "The most iconically recognizable tree in Japan, the cherry blossom tree – Prunus serrulata – is a medium-sized tree that traditionally blooms with white and pink flowers in the spring.\n\nThe tree naturally grows across Southeast Asia; however, due to its beauty, it is commonly planted around the world. One significant location is Washington, D.C., which has a large number of cherry blossom trees donated by the country of Japan that bloom each year. In Japan, some of the most popular locations to view the cherry blossoms include Ueno Park, Takato Castle Ruins Park, and Mount Yoshinoyama.\n\nThere are three particularly famous cherry trees in Japan. The first, Yamataka Jindai Zakura, is the oldest and largest cherry tree in the country, estimated to be over 2,000 years old. The other two are Miharu Takizakura, located in Fukushima Prefecture and over 1,000 years old; and Neodani Usuzumizakura, located in Gifu Prefecture and estimated to be over 1,500 years old."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_TREEBUDDHISTPINE"   ;   Str = "Pine trees are among the four families of coniferous plants. Most conifers are evergreens, but continually shed needles throughout the year. All conifers are gymnosperms, which means that they produce seeds (like pinecones) that have no fruit and are usually dispersed by wind rather than by animals. Many pinecones have evolved to only sprout following a forest fire, so as to ensure that there will be sufficient light to feed the growing sapling.\n\nTemperate coniferous forests grow in regions with sandy soil, mild winters and warm summers, and plenty of rain. They contain some of the tallest trees on the planet, but have relatively low animal diversity. Conifers growing in these forests include firs and giants such as the sequoia and redwood.\n\nFurther towards the poles are the boreal or taiga forests, where winters are very cold and summers are short and cool. Conifers in these forests include firs and spruces. Animal diversity there is even lower than in temperate forests."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_TREEBUDDHISTPINESNOW"   ;   Str = "Pine trees are among the four families of coniferous plants. Most conifers are evergreens, but continually shed needles throughout the year. All conifers are gymnosperms, which means that they produce seeds (like pinecones) that have no fruit and are usually dispersed by wind rather than by animals. Many pinecones have evolved to only sprout following a forest fire, so as to ensure that there will be sufficient light to feed the growing sapling.\n\nTemperate coniferous forests grow in regions with sandy soil, mild winters and warm summers, and plenty of rain. They contain some of the tallest trees on the planet, but have relatively low animal diversity. Conifers growing in these forests include firs and giants such as the sequoia and redwood.\n\nFurther towards the poles are the boreal or taiga forests, where winters are very cold and summers are short and cool. Conifers in these forests include firs and spruces. Animal diversity there is even lower than in temperate forests."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_TREEMAPLE"   ;   Str = "The Japanese maple, or Acer palmatum, is a small deciduous tree with hand-shaped leaves – hence the Latin name – that has become popular in landscaping around the world due to its uniquely dark purple or red foliage.\n\nOriginally found on the edges of Japanese forests, the tree has been cultivated in Japan for centuries. In 1820, when the Swedish botanist Carl Thunberg was traveling in Japan, he was so taken with the tree that he brought it back to England. This led to a surge in its popularity, and by the 20th century, it had become a staple in gardens worldwide.\n\nThese maples hold a place in Japanese cultural memory. In the 10th century, the imperial regent Fujiwara no Tadahira wrote the following poem after being deeply moved by the vibrant colors of the maples:\n\n“The maples of Mount Ogura, / If they could understand, / Would keep their brilliant leaves, until / The Ruler of this land / Pass with his royal band.”"
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_TREEPINEBROWN"   ;   Str = "Pine trees are among the four families of coniferous plants. Most conifers are evergreens, but continually shed needles throughout the year. All conifers are gymnosperms, which means that they produce seeds (like pinecones) that have no fruit and are usually dispersed by wind rather than by animals. Many pinecones have evolved to only sprout following a forest fire, so as to ensure that there will be sufficient light to feed the growing sapling.\n\nTemperate coniferous forests grow in regions with sandy soil, mild winters and warm summers, and plenty of rain. They contain some of the tallest trees on the planet, but have relatively low animal diversity. Conifers growing in these forests include firs and giants such as the sequoia and redwood.\n\nFurther towards the poles are the boreal or taiga forests, where winters are very cold and summers are short and cool. Conifers in these forests include firs and spruces. Animal diversity there is even lower than in temperate forests.\n\nMagumo has cursed this pine tree, causing it to turn into a creepy orange color."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_SNOWMONKEY"   ;   Str = "Scientific name -- Macaca fuscata\nSize -- 19-25 lbs.\nDiet -- omnivorous\n\nThe snow monkey, or Japanese macaque, is endemic to Japan. Typically found in deciduous and evergreen forests, it has light brown fur and a red face. On average, males are slightly larger than females, weighing about 25 pounds compared to 19 pounds. Snow monkeys live in a matrilineal society, where females generally hold more social power within the kinship group than males. Highly intelligent, these monkeys are particularly fond of sneaking into hot springs across Japan to enjoy a warm bath during the cold winter months."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_JAPANESEWOLF"   ;   Str = "Scientific name -- Canis lupus hodophilax\nSize -- 50-150 lbs.\nDiet -- deer, caribou, elk, cattle, small mammals\n\nThe Japanese wolf, a now-extinct subspecies of wolf, was last officially sighted in Nara Prefecture in 1905 CE. Wolves hold a significant place in Japanese mythology—one of the most well-known tales is that of the senbiki ōkami, a mythical pack of wolves said to roam mountain roads at night.\n\nAlthough once revered in Japanese culture, the Japanese wolf was driven to extinction by a combination of rabies outbreaks and human poaching during the Meiji Restoration period. Some reports claim that the wolf may still survive in Japan's most remote regions, but no conclusive evidence has been found to support these sightings."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_SEROW"   ;   Str = "Scientific name -- Capricornus crispus\nSize -- 66-99 lbs.\nDiet -- acorns, leaves, and plant shoots\n\nThe serow is a unique hoofed mammal endemic to three of Japan’s main islands: Honshū, Shikoku, and Kyūshū. Often described as a ‘goat-like antelope’ due to its small, curved horns, the serow stands about 32 inches tall on average and can weigh up to 100 pounds. Serows hold a long-standing place in Japanese cultural memory, with texts dating back to the Heian period referencing their otherworldly presence in the mountains. Regarded as a 'living national treasure,' they are legally protected in Japan."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_GREENPHEASANT"   ;   Str = "Scientific name -- Phasianus versicolor\nSize -- 1-7 lbs.\nDiet -- worms, insects, grains, and plants\n\nThe Green Pheasant is native to Japan and serves as the country’s national bird. With their distinct plumage – dark green with red faces for males, and light brown for females – and a unique call that echoes the emotional laments often found in Japanese poetry, these majestic birds have thriven in Japan for thousands of years."
// End of Japanese Nature History

This peaceful meadow endures for a moderate period of time, though it can be destroyed by other god powers. Arcadian Meadows must be placed at a distance from each other.\n\nTo unveil a meadow, select the Arcadian Meadow icon from the God Power Menu <icon=\"(32)($TextGamepadLeftTrigger)\"> + <icon=\"(32)($TextGamepadFaceLeft)\">, then press <icon=\"(32)($TextGamepadFaceBottom)\"> on the ground where you want it to bloom."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_ARCHAICAGEDEMETER"   ;   Str = "Demeter, one of the twelve Olympians and among the most revered goddesses of ancient Greece, reigned over harvest and agriculture. Celebrated far and wide as the bringer of food and fertility, her symbols of wheat, poppies, and the cornucopia not only presided over times of plenty but also served as poignant reminders of the cycles of life and death.\n\nHer most famous story involves the kidnapping of her beloved daughter, Persephone, by Hades, who intended to marry her. Upon discovering Persephone's disappearance, Demeter embarked on a relentless search across the world. Consumed by grief, she neglected her divine duties, halting the growth of life and plunging the earth into a devastating famine. Witnessing the unfolding catastrophe, Zeus commanded Hades to release Persephone. However, having consumed food in the Underworld, Persephone was bound by the decree of the Fates to remain there part of the year. It was decided that she would spend half the year with her mother and half with her husband, thus explaining the changing seasons. During the winter months, when Persephone is in the Underworld, Demeter's sorrow causes the earth to become barren."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_CLASSICALAGEPAN"   ;   Str = "Pan, the god of the wild, lived on the edge between civilization and the wilderness. An ancient nature spirit, he reveled in a carefree life, associated with groves, woodlands, fertility, and spring. Constantly accompanied by the nymphs of the forest, he delighted them with his song and merry manner. Known for his mischievous pranks, Pan would often leap out at travelers, screaming horribly to fill them with fear. During the Titanomachy, he used this same technique to scatter the enemies of the gods.\n\nProne to love and strong emotions, Pan once encountered Syrinx, a nymph in the Arcadian woods. Instantly infatuated, he chased her as she fled. When Syrinx reached her sisters, they transformed her into a reed and hid her among other reeds by a river. Unable to discern which reed she had become, Pan cut seven pieces and tied them together, blowing through them to make music. This instrument, now known as a pan flute, became his constant companion, which he carried with him at all times, playing music that echoed his longing and love."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_COMMUNALHEARTH"   ;   Str = "Hestia's God Power embodies the spirit of hospitality by constructing a Communal Hearth, a warm and inviting banquet that inspires your Villagers to work harder (increasing gather rate) and entices enemy units to join your cause with its cozy atmosphere.\n\nTo summon the structure, left click on the Communal Hearth icon, then left click on the desired location on the map."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_COMMUNALHEARTH_ALTFORGAMEPAD"   ;   Str = "Hestia's God Power embodies the spirit of hospitality by constructing a Communal Hearth, a warm and inviting banquet that inspires your Villagers to work harder (increasing gather rate) and entices enemy units to join your cause with its cozy atmosphere. \n\nTo summon the structure, select the Communal Hearth icon from the God Power Menu <icon=\"(32)($TextGamepadLeftTrigger)\"> + <icon=\"(32)($TextGamepadFaceLeft)\">, then press <icon=\"(32)($TextGamepadFaceBottom)\"> on the desired map location."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_DIVINELABOR" ; Str = "Demeter, known as the Grain-Mother, was the revered goddess who provided grain for bread and blessed all harvesters. As crops sprang from the earth, she was also honored as an earth goddess. Highly esteemed by the Greeks, Demeter was the focus of numerous major cults and festivals, such as the Eleusinian Mysteries and the Thesmophoria. Her blessings were seen as essential for sustaining civilization, making her a fundamental pillar in the lives of those who toiled in the fields."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_ENCHANTEDHYMN"   ;   Str = "The sirens' song was a force of unparalleled power, irresistibly drawing those who heard it. Many sailors, entranced by the sirens' haunting hymn, leapt into the sea or steered their ships to destruction. Such was the sirens' ruthlessness and the beauty of their song that Circe reserved her most urgent warning for Odysseus, imploring him not to listen to their enchanting melody."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_FATEDARROWS"   ;   Str = "In Greek mythology, the gods were not distant deities—they actively shaped the destinies of mortals, often quite dramatically. During the Trojan War divine intervention was constant and decisive. The goddess Athena, favoring the Greeks, frequently manipulated battles, while Aphrodite, supporting the Trojans, famously saved Paris from death by whisking him away in a cloud during a duel with Menelaus. These divine interferences weren’t just acts of favoritism; they reflected the gods’ personalities, rivalries, and whims, turning mortal lives into battlegrounds for divine pride, vengeance, and conflict."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_GRACIOUSHOSPITALITY" ; Str = "In ancient Greek culture, xenia, or hospitality, was a central aspect of society and a measure of a person's virtue. Many myths begin with a hero welcoming a stranger into their home, only for the stranger to be a god. Hospitality was frequently symbolized by the hearth, which was essential for warmth, cooking, and completing sacrifices for the gods. As such, Hestia, the goddess of the hearth, always received the first offering at every domestic sacrifice."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_HALLOWEDWOODLANDS"   ;   Str = "Within the vast forested areas of ancient Greece, there were locales of considerable spirituality believed to be under the protection of a god. These sacred lands were marked off from common uses and were seldom entered. Those who ventured in peacefully were considered to be under the protection of the gods and the creatures within."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_HEROICAGEHESTIA"   ;   Str = "As the firstborn to Kronos and Rheia, Hestia was the first to be consumed by the Titan and the last to be purged from him by Zeus, earning her the title of both the eldest and the youngest Olympian. As the goddess of the hearth and home, she was always the first to be honored whenever there was a sacrifice to the gods. A virgin goddess who never married, she rejected the advances of both Poseidon and Apollo, choosing to remain chaste.\n\nA pacifist who welcomed all, Hestia famously refused to take sides during the Trojan War. Even during the Gigantomachy, she did not participate in combat, instead contributing by keeping the home fires burning while the other gods fought. Modest, welcoming, and understanding, she reminds us that gods don’t need to be violent to be powerful and they don’t need to be warriors to be revered."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_IRONGRIP"   ;   Str = "The harpies were infamous for their habit of swooping down to steal food. Cruel, vicious, and perpetually hungry, their diet wasn't limited to mere sustenance. It is said that they would occasionally snatch people and carry them to the Erinyes. This occurred so frequently that it became common parlance to say that when someone suddenly disappeared, they had been 'carried off by the harpies'."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_LOTUS_EATERS" ; Str = "In Homer’s Odyssey, as Odysseus strives to find his way home, he encounters the land of the Lotus-eaters. This peaceful civilization welcomes his crew, offering them fresh water and edible fruits and flowers. The lotus was \"sweet as honey,\" yet it induces a state of blissful apathy in those who consume it, causing Odysseus' men to forget their troubles, families, and desire to return home. It is only through Odysseus' iron will that he manages to force his sailors back onto their ships and continue their journey."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_PANS_PIONEERS" ; Str = "In the groves of Arcadia, Pan gathered a devoted following. These Arcadian shepherds ventured beyond their homeland, spreading his worship to Athens and beyond. Their journeys echoed a deeper Greek impulse: the relentless pursuit of knowledge and expansion. As Pan’s followers roamed, they became unlikely pioneers, blending tradition with a curiosity that fueled Greek exploration and cultural growth."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_MYTHICAGEPERSEPHONE"   ;   Str = "The dreaded and revered Queen of the Underworld didn’t always hold that title. As the daughter of Demeter, she was gathering flowers one day when Hades burst through the ground to abduct her. In love with her and with Zeus' permission, he intended to make her his bride. Demeter was furious, transforming Persephone’s companions into half-bird Sirens as punishment for allowing the abduction. Demeter searched the world for her daughter, neglecting her duties as the goddess of harvest and halting the earth's fertility. As famine spread throughout the world, Zeus ordered Hades to return Persephone. Hades, knowing the Fates had decreed that anyone who tasted food in the Underworld could not leave, gave her a pomegranate from which she ate six seeds. Thus, for six months of every year, she would have to stay in the Underworld, during which winter would reign. Come spring, she would return to live with her mother, and all nature would flourish once more.\n\nOver time, Persephone embraced her role as Queen of the Underworld, helping to maintain the balance between life and death. As both a goddess of spring and of the dead, she was worshipped through secret rituals by numerous cults eager to gain favor in the afterlife."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_PIOUSSACRIFICE"   ;   Str = "After being kidnapped by Hades and later recovered by Demeter, Persephone became a powerful symbol bridging life and death. Eager to seek rewards in the afterlife, the ancient Greeks participated in yearly initiations into the cult of Persephone and Demeter, known as the Eleusinian Mysteries. This secretive cult shrouded its rites, ceremonies, and beliefs in mystery, accessible only to the initiated who were bound by a solemn vow of secrecy."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_PREDATORYINSTINCT"   ;   Str = "Despite his cruelty, King Lykaon and his descendants were renowned heroes for the people of Arcadia. Known for their ferocity, they were both feared and revered. With over 50 sons, King Lykaon's lineage founded numerous cities, established many festivals (including the famous Lycaean Games), and dedicated countless temples to the gods. This fervor for civilization and cultural development starkly contrasted with their ruthless and violent nature."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_SACREDLAND" ; Str = "Far from being solely a figure of death Persephone also embodied the renewal of life. Each year, her return from the underworld to reunite with her mother, Demeter, heralded the arrival of spring and the rebirth of the land. This cyclical journey was not just a seasonal metaphor—it was a sacred symbol of life’s eternal rhythm. In the Eleusinian Mysteries, one of ancient Greece’s most revered religious rites, Persephone’s ascent from the underworld was celebrated as a promise of immortality and spiritual renewal. Her myth offered hope that life could emerge from death, and that the soul, like the seed, could rise again."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECHS_THORNEDWALLS" ; Str = "As a rustic god, Pan existed in the boundary between civilization and the wilderness. He was not commonly worshipped in temples, but instead in natural settings such as the Cave of Pan. Rural communities revered him as a guardian, especially protective of shepherds and their flocks, who would incite great terror on those who would disrupt the simple way of life."

ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_COMMUNALHEARTH"   ;   Str = "For the Greeks, the hearth lay at the center of the family, the community, and the state. Just as every household had its hearth, so did every seat of government, whether in a village or a city. It was the sacred duty of the chieftain and their family to tend to this hearth. Though it might be ritually extinguished at night, if it ever faded unintentionally, it was considered a grave breach of duty towards the community as a whole."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_HAMADRYAD"   ;   Str = "Scientific name -- Nymphaea arborea.\nSize -- Human sized (Nymph) / 40-80' tall (Tree).\nDiet -- Water, Sunlight.\n\nUnlike other nymphs Hamadryads are intrinsically linked to a particular tree. They are born bonded to the tree, and if the tree dies, so do they. One cannot exist without the other. Generally gentle, shy and nurturing, they are fiercely protective of their tree and the forest they live in."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_HARPY"   ;   Str = "Scientific name -- Ornithandra harpyia.\nSize -- Human sized.\nDiet -- Omnivorous, will often steal food from people or just eat them.\n\nHarpies, whose name means \"snatcher,\" are winged female creatures often portrayed as half-human, half-bird, who served as agents of punishment by the gods. They are the personification of storms and are frequently depicted stealing food or carrying people away to torment them for angering a deity. The most famous episode involving a Harpy is their torment of King Phineus, where they relentlessly stole his food and harassed him."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_SIREN"   ;   Str = "Scientific name -- Nymphaea Sirenica.\nSize -- 8'2”.\nDiet -- Fish, sailors.\n\nAlthough closely related to marine birds, these sea nymphs possess a human-like appearance and an enchanting voice used to mesmerize their prey. Sirens were once Persephone's companions, until Demeter, furious at her daughter's abduction, transformed them into man-eating monsters. Clever, distinguished, and alluring, they usually conceal their monstrous features until the opportune moment to strike."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_ICARUS"   ;   Str = "Age -- 19\nHomeland -- Athens\nOccupation -- Unemployed, son of famous inventor\n\nThe son of the genius craftsman Daedalus, Icarus embodied youthful daring and curiosity, often to the point of overconfidence and recklessness. When Theseus escaped the labyrinth designed by Daedalus, King Minos of Crete was so enraged that he imprisoned Daedalus and Icarus within the labyrinth. Using feathers that fell from birds flying overhead, Daedalus fashioned a pair of wings for their escape. Before their daring attempt, he warned his son not to fly too close to the sea, to avoid the water soaking the feathers, nor too close to the sun, as the beeswax holding the feathers in place would melt. They launched their escape, but midway through, filled with the exhilaration of flight, Icarus soared higher and higher. Soon, the wax began to melt and the wings fell apart. Icarus plummeted into the ocean and drowned in the waves."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_IOLAUS"   ;   Str = "Age -- 23\nHomeland -- Thebes\nOccupation -- charioteer & squire\n\nCousin to Heracles, Iolaus was his unwavering companion throughout the twelve labors. He drove Heracles' chariot, carried his weapons, and fought valiantly by his side. During the second labor, Iolaus proved indispensable in the battle against the fearsome Lernaean Hydra. As the monster grew two heads for each one Heracles severed, the hero was quickly overwhelmed. With quick thinking and bravery, Iolaus leapt into action, cauterizing the necks as soon as Heracles beheaded them. After an intense and grueling fight, only the final head remained. Together, they struck the decisive blow, and the monstrous Hydra was defeated."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_LYKAON"   ;   Str = "Scientific name -- Lupus lykaonius.\nSize -- 7' tall.\nDiet -- carnivore, any meat within range.\n\nKing Lykaon was the legendary ruler of Arcadia. Famous for his cruelty, impiety and arrogance, he attempted to test the omniscience of Zeus by inviting him to a feast and serving his own son Nyctimus. Zeus, in a outpouring of rage and disgust, transformed Lykaon into a wolf and restored Nyctimus to life."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_MIDAS"   ;   Str = "Age -- 51\nHomeland -- Phrygia\nOccupation -- King\n\nLegendary King of Phrygia, Midas was known for his immense wealth and ambition. One day, while walking through his rose garden, he found a drunken satyr sleeping. Recognizing him as Silenus, the foster father of the god Dionysus, Midas treated him hospitably and, after eleven days, returned him to Dionysus. Grateful, Dionysus offered Midas any wish as a reward. Driven by greed, Midas asked for the power to turn anything he touched into gold. At first, he delighted in his new ability, transforming much of his household, including every rose in his garden, into gold. He ordered a feast to celebrate, but upon taking the first bite, he discovered that food and water also turned to gold upon touching his lips. Desperate, Midas prayed to Dionysus to lift what had become a curse. The god heard his plea and instructed him to wash in the river Pactolus, which washed away his power and turned the river sands to gold."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_ORPHEUS"   ;   Str = "Age -- 28\nHomeland -- Thrace\nOccupation -- hero and demigod\n\nInheriting his artistic gifts from his mother, the muse Calliope, Orpheus created songs so enthralling that all living things stopped to listen, and even stones were charmed by his music. He joined Jason and the Argonauts, saving the ship from the Sirens by playing his lyre more beautifully than the monsters, overpowering their bewitching song. His most famous adventure, however, is his journey to the underworld in search of his lost love, Eurydice. Using his music to calm Cerberus and gain entry to the underworld, he played his lyre before Hades and Persephone. They were so moved that they allowed Eurydice to return to the world of the living, on the condition that Orpheus walked in front of her and did not look back on the way out. As they neared the exit, Orpheus, in his excitement, turned back to see Eurydice, causing her to disappear back into the underworld forever."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_AMAZONARCHER"   ;   Str = "The Amazon Archer is an elite long-range warrior trained at the Fortress, deadly against infantry and capable of wearing down buildings from afar. Gifted with exceptional sight and precision, they dominate the battlefield at distance but are vulnerable to swift cavalry and sudden assaults. Only ten may be trained at a time.\n\nThe Amazons sworn to Demeter are sacred guardians, trained during the Mythic Age to defend the harvest from all who would despoil it. Their discipline allows them to concentrate fire upon key targets, breaking advancing infantry lines and weakening enemy footholds. Though lightly armored compared to front-line soldiers, their strength lies in distance, patience, and positioning. So long as they hold their ground and the enemy remains at range, the lands of Demeter remain under vigilant watch."
