// Aztec History
ID = "STR_HISTORY_AZTEC"   ;   Str = "The Aztecs are a civilization of speed, ferocity, and spiritual power. As masters of both harvest and terror, they draw enemies in, ambushing them to feed their rituals. As warriors fall, their tonalli, or life force, is released. The stronger the foe, the more tonalli they hold, ripe for the picking. This tonalli is gathered by Warrior Priests and religious buildings including Temples, Great Temples and Tzompantlis.\n\nBeyond the chaos of combat, ritual sacrifice fuels the Aztecs' divine economy. Settlers may be offered at the Temple to create surges of Favor. In later ages, entire groups of units can be sacrificed at the Great Temple, unlocking unique and powerful perks.\n\nTo compensate for their weaker mid-game armies, the Aztecs rely on guerrilla tactics. They employ deception to control the field. Quimichin Spies and Ocelotl Warriors can vanish into stealth, holding key positions unseen and striking when least expected. Defensively, the Aztecs replace traditional towers with the terrifying Tzompantli. These skull towers hurl projectiles at their foe, and when enhanced, radiate dread itself, stunning enemies as terror takes hold."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_OM"   ;   Str = "Aztlan, Aztecan utopia, is destroyed when the eternal fighting between Quetzalcoatl the Wise and Tezcatlipoca the Deceiver, reaches a fever pitch. Huitzilopochtli, the fierce God of War, is entrusted to anoint a divine leader and guide his people through the dangers of the Mexican valley to establish a new capital. Meanwhile, Tezcatlipoca escapes through his Obsidian Mirror, traveling across the world to recruit mythology's fiercest warriors to bring back to Mexico and reignite war with his brothers. Unlikely alliances must be forged and broken in order to restore order to the heavens and ensure a future for the Aztec people."

// Gods
ID = "STR_HISTORY_GOD_ARCHAICAGEQUETZALCOATL"   ;   Str = "Quetzalcoatl, known as the Feathered Serpent, is the god of wind, wisdom, learning, and creation. Quetzalcoatl played an important role in the creation of humans and was seen as a protector of civilization and culture. He is connected to the planet Venus and often depicted as a serpent with feathers, symbolizing the link between earth and sky."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_GOD_ARCHAICAGETEZCATLIPOCA"   ;   Str = "Tezcatlipoca is a powerful and multifaceted deity associated with night, sorcery, the sky, and destiny. His name, translated as 'Smoking Mirror', reflects his link to obsidian, often used for mirrors and sacred rituals. Tezcatlipoca played a central role in the cycles of creation and destruction, often stood in opposition to Quetzalcoatl. One could say their rivalry shaped the world. Mysterious and unpredictable, Tezcatlipoca inspired both fear and reverence among his followers."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_GOD_ARCHAICAGEHUITZILOPOCHTLI"   ;   Str = "Huitzilopochtli is revered as the god of the sun and war, and the divine figure of his people. Unlike many Mesoamerican gods who were shared across regions, he was uniquely and definitively Aztec. His name means “Hummingbird of the South,” and he was believed to have guided his people to the site where they founded Tenochtitlán.\n\nAccording to myth, he defeated his sister Coyolxauhqui and cast her from a sacred mountain, a story later reflected in ritual sacrifice. As the sun god, Huitzilopochtli depended on human offerings to sustain his power and ensure the sun’s victory over darkness, symbolizing both Aztec military strength and cultural identity."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_GOD_CLASSICALAGEPATECATL"   ;   Str = "Patecatl is a deity connected with healing, fertility, and medicinal wisdom. He is also revered as the god of pulque, a fermented beverage made from the maguey (agave) plant. Known as the discoverer of healing herbs, he is celebrated as a guardian of health. Patecatl is husband to Mayahuel, the goddess of the maguey plant, and father to the Centzon Totochtin, the four hundred divine rabbits associated with intoxication and festivity."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_GOD_CLASSICALAGEMALINALXOCHITL"   ;   Str = "Malinalxochitl is a goddess associated with witchcraft, serpents, and the desert. She is the sister of Huitzilopochtli, god of sun and war. Malinalxochitl is known as a powerful leader and sorceress who, during their search for a new homeland, was abandoned by her followers at her brother's command. Later, she became the mother of Copil. \n\nMalinalxochitl embodies themes of power, magic, and betrayal, symbolizing both strength and the consequences of divine conflict."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_GOD_CLASSICALAGEHUEHUECOYOTL"   ;   Str = "Huehuecóyotl is a deity of music, dance, mischief, and storytelling. His name translates to 'Old Coyote', fitting his combination of human and coyote traits. He is often portrayed as a trickster embodying both humor and unpredictability. Huehuecóyotl is a vessel of joy and creativity, while not shying away from deception and chaos. Huehuecóyotl is also associated with festivity, transformation, and the playful side of the divine, representing a balance between disorder and artistic expression. This balance is what makes him a lively and layered figure in ancient tales."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_GOD_HEROICAGECOATLICUE"   ;   Str = "Coatlicue is a formidable and revered goddess known as Mother of the Gods. Her name, translated to 'She of the Serpent Skirt', reflects her deep connection to the earth, fertility, life, and death. She became miraculously pregnant with Huitzilopochtli when a ball of feathers descended into her lap. Enraged by this, her other children sought to destroy her, but Huitzilopochtli was born fully armed and vanquished them. Coatlicue represents the balance between creation and destruction; the nurturing and devouring aspects of the earth's power."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_GOD_HEROICAGECOYOLXAUHQUI"   ;   Str = "Coyolxauhqui is a goddess associated with the moon, whose name translates to 'Painted with Bells'. She is the daughter of Coatlicue and the sister of Huitzilopochtli. Coyolxauhqui led her brothers, the Centzon Huitznahua (the Four Hundred Southern Stars) in an uprising against their mother Coatlicue, after she mysteriously became pregnant. Huitzilopochtli, born fully armed, defeated them in battle, decapitating Coyolxauhqui and casting her body down a mountainside. This story represents the triumph of the sun over the moon and stars, and her fragmented likeness was immortalized at the base of the Templo Mayor in Tenochtitlán in a grand stone carving."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_GOD_HEROICAGEITZPAPALOTL"   ;   Str = "Itzpapalotl is a formidable and enigmatic goddess whose name translates to 'Obsidian Butterfly'. She is linked to war, death, the night, and the stars. Itzpapalotl is often portrayed as a skeletal warrior adorned with butterfly or bat wings edged in obsidian blades. As ruler of Tamoanchan, a paradise for the souls of infants who died at birth, she represents both ferocity and renewal. Despite her fearsome demeanour, Itzpapalotl also represents rebirth and transformation. She reflects the delicate balance between beauty and dread, life and death."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_GOD_MYTHICAGETLALOC"   ;   Str = "Tlaloc is a powerful deity of rain, water, and fertility. He is revered for his control over the elements that sustain agriculture and life. Often depicted with goggle-like eyes and fangs, he embodies the might of storms and lightning. Though capable of granting nourishing rains, he was equally feared for unleashing floods, droughts, and hail. To gain his favor and ensure abundant harvests, people offered prayers, gifts, and sacrifices, seeking to balance his dual nature as both a giver and destroyer of life."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_GOD_MYTHICAGEXOLOTL"   ;   Str = "Xolotl is a deity connected with fire, lightning, and the underworld. He is the twin brother of Quetzalcoatl and often depicted with the head of a dog. Xolotl serves as a guide for souls, ensuring the dead safely reach Mictlan; their final resting place. He is closely associated to transformation, twins, and physical mutations, most famously the Axolotl, a creature believed to embody his ability to change form. Xolotl played a central role in myths that explore the cycles of life, death and rebirth."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_GOD_MYTHICAGEMICTLANTECUTLI"   ;   Str = "Mictlantecuhtli is the god of death and the underworld, ruling over Mictlan alongside his wife, Mictecacihuatl. Often depicted as a skeletal figure decorated with bones and skulls, he oversees the afterlife. As powerful and fearsome deity, Mictlantecuhtli is associated with darkness and decay. Despite his grim nature, he plays a crucial role in maintaining the natural cycle of life and death."

// Units
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_VILLAGERAZTEC"   ;   Str = "Settlers form the basis of your economy. They gather resources in a typical fashion and deposit them at upgradable Calpullis when they reach their carry capacity. \n\nMake sure you have enough Settlers to maintain a steady flow of resources, but remember, they are weak in combat. Aztec Settlers can be expended to generate Favor through Devotion. To help the Aztecs keep pace with the expanding economies of other civilizations, Settlers are slightly cheaper than other villagers. The Chinampas Farm upgrade increases their Food production speed."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_CARAVANAZTEC"   ;   Str = "Trade was vital to the strength and stability of the Aztec Empire. While the central valleys were rich in agricultural products, other goods, like obsidian, cacao, feathers, and cotton were imported from distant regions.\n\nWhat makes this system remarkable is that it functioned without beasts of burden or wheeled transport. Human porters carried goods across vast distances, allowing trade networks to span much of Mesoamerica. Luxury items like cacao traveled hundreds of miles, demonstrating the efficiency and reach of Aztec trade.\n\nAztec merchants, known as Pochtecatl, traveled vast distances to exchange goods and gather intelligence. Markets, such as Tlatelolco, bustled with activity, drawing thousands of traders daily. Trade not only sustained the empire’s economy but also helped extend its influence far beyond the Valley of Mexico."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_QUIMICHIN_SPY"   ;   Str = "Drawing from ancient traditions of stealth and subterfuge, these spies rely on their speed and cunning.\n\nQuimichin Spies are agile scout units that can enter stealth within range of a treeline. But be careful. Other scout types and towers can see their silhouettes at a distance."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_TEQUIHUA_ARCHER"   ;   Str = "Tequihua Archers are young warriors still proving themselves in battle, often given the role of harassing enemy lines with ranged attacks while the main force closed in. \n\nTequihua Archers are a cheap early ranged unit used to counter enemy infantry. To help them keep up with the Tlamanih's movements, they have above average mobility. Their attack range can also make them a decent defensive option when strategically positioned behind terrain or buildings."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_EAGLE_WARRIOR"   ;   Str = "In Aztec warfare, Eagle Warriors embodied both nobility and lethality; warriors who struck before the enemy could even prepare.\n\nEagle Warriors are powerful, mobile ranged generalists effective against most human units. With high attack range, speed, and mobility, they excel at kiting. Unlike more specialized units, they perform well without strict counter-matchup planning, and upgrades further boost their speed and damage."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_TLAMANIH_SPEARMAN"   ;   Str = "Armed with spears and courage, Tlamanih carried out the will of the empire, often forming the first wave of any Aztec assault. \n\nTlamanih are fast, cost-effective spearmen who form the backbone of the Aztecs’ early military. Their mobility and early availability make them ideal for raids and early skirmishes."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_COYOTE_WARRIOR"   ;   Str = "The Aztecs lack traditional cavalry, but Coyote Warriors help fill this role as swift infantry units that excel against ranged enemies. \n\nAvailable early on, Coyote Warriors can become significantly more effective with the right upgrades and myth technologies. Specializing in them supports aggressive playstyles during the early and mid-game, but they begin to fall off in the late game, struggling against fortified positions and heavier enemy units."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_OCELOTL_WARRIOR"   ;   Str = "Ocelotl Warriors were revered as elite Aztec fighters, embodying the fierce spirit and agility of the jaguar itself.\n\nOcelotl Warriors are versatile mid-game infantry known for their high damage, mobility, and stealth. Furthermore, they can leap at enemies, dealing bonus damage and on-hit effects.\n\nOcelotl Warriors automatically hide when close to trees, allowing them to excel at flanking. Ambushing opponents by leaping from stealth causes them to unleash a flurry of fast attacks as they take their opponents by surprise. But be careful. Scout types and towers can see their silhouettes at a distance."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_OTONTIN"   ;   Str = "Wielding heavy weapons and clad in sturdy armor, the Otontin Smasher embodies the might needed to breach even the toughest defenses.\n\nWith a bonus to damage against buildings, the Otontin Smasher helps compensate for the Aztecs' limited siege capabilities. Although they are costly to train, this investment allows the Aztecs to reduce their dependence on Myth units for sieging.\n\nOtontin Smashers are still effective against other units as a high durability infantry option. However, like most siege-oriented infantry, they move slower than other Aztec units. Their bulkiness allows them to push through enemy lines and survive sustained attacks from buildings while breaking down fortifications."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_SHORN_ONE"   ;   Str = "The Shorn Ones are the highest ranking warriors of the Aztecs. They are good against most enemies, but especially strong at taking out enemy infantry. \n\nShorn Ones excel at spearheading late game assaults, tearing through enemy front lines, and holding defensive positions. Their prestige as the ultimate Aztec warrior comes with a high resource cost, population cost, and training time. They are few on the battlefield but pose a threat the enemy cannot afford to ignore."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_JAGUAR_RIDER"   ;   Str = "Jaguar Riders are the sole cavalry-class unit in the Aztec roster. With strong combat stats, they perform well against most human units.\n\nLike Ocelotl Warriors, they can enter stealth, making them the fastest and most mobile stealth units available. Their ambush attacks allow them to leap from the shadows, unleashing a flurry of fast attacks as they take their enemies by surprise. But be careful. Scout types and towers can see their silhouettes at a distance."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_WARRIOR_PRIEST"   ;   Str = "Warrior Priests are the common Aztec hero unit. They collect tonalli, dropped by defeated units, to supplement favour generation, and can construct religious buildings. They fight at close range and are especially strong against myth units given their Hero status, but they are also effective against human units. \n\nWarrior Priests are necessary in any Aztec strategy, as they reduce reliance on the Teixiptla as the sole myth unit counter and contribute to Favor generation through tonalli absorption."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_TEIXIPTLA_HUITZ"   ;   Str = "Huitzilopochtli is the Aztec god of war, the sun, and conquest. He embodies martial strength and divine leadership, guiding the Aztecs through battle and expansion.\n\nEach Major God can temporarily possess one Warrior Priest at a time as their Teixiptla, granting them immense power. The Teixiptla’s abilities and appearance depend on which Major God is being played. The Teixiptla deals significant damage with special attacks, making them a potent tool but also a prime target for enemies. As a Hero, the Teixiptla deals bonus damage to myth units and gains bonus stats with each Age.\n\nAfter a short time, the Teixiptla’s mortal frame can no longer contain the power of the god possessing them, and the unit is lost. Players will have to choose when to invoke this power decisively."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_TEIXIPTLA_QUETZ"   ;   Str = "Quetzalcoatl is the Aztec god of wisdom, wind, and creation. Known as the Feathered Serpent, he represents knowledge, learning, and the balance between nature and civilization.\n\nEach Major God can temporarily possess one Warrior Priest at a time as their Teixiptla, granting them immense power. The Teixiptla’s abilities and appearance depend on which Major God is being played. The Teixiptla deals significant damage with special attacks, making them a potent tool but also a prime target for enemies. As a Hero, the Teixiptla deals bonus damage to myth units and gains bonus stats with each Age.\n\nAfter a short time, the Teixiptla’s mortal frame can no longer contain the power of the god possessing them, and the unit is lost. Players will have to choose when to invoke this power decisively."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_TEIXIPTLA_TEZCA"   ;   Str = "Tezcatlipoca is the Aztec god of night, sorcery, and destiny. Mysterious and powerful, he embodies chaos, conflict, and the unseen forces that shape fate and ambition.\n\nEach Major God can temporarily possess one Warrior Priest at a time as their Teixiptla, granting them immense power. The Teixiptla’s abilities and appearance depend on which Major God is being played. The Teixiptla deals significant damage with special attacks, making them a potent tool but also a prime target for enemies. As a Hero, the Teixiptla deals bonus damage to myth units and gains bonus stats with each Age.\n\nAfter a short time, the Teixiptla’s mortal frame can no longer contain the power of the god possessing them, and the unit is lost. Players will have to choose when to invoke this power decisively."
// Myth Units
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_TITANAZTEC"   ;   Str = "Scientific name -- Deity defies classification \nSize -- 100' tall \nDiet -- Worship and dominance \n\nIn a dark ritual, Tezcatlipoca offers his own foot as a sacrifice to summon the primordial being Cipactli, the Aztec Titan, a colossal manifestation of divine power. The creature embodies the raw, primal forces that shaped the world before human memory. \n\nAn unstoppable force that tramples armies, razes cities, and warps the battlefield with its presence. // Naval Units
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_FISHINGSHIPAZTEC"   ;   Str = "An Aztec fishing ship was a small wooden canoe, usually made from a hollowed-out tree trunk. It had a flat bottom for navigating shallow lakes and canals around Tenochtitlán. Propelled by paddles or poles, it typically carried 1–3 fishermen.\n\nFishing tools included nets, lines with bone hooks, and spears. These canoes were used in lakes like Texcoco and Xochimilco to catch fish, shrimp, axolotls, and insect larvae. Though simple, they played a vital role in food supply, trade, and tribute in the Aztec Empire."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_TRANSPORTSHIPAZTEC"   ;   Str = "An Aztec transport ship was a large wooden canoe, used to move goods and people across the lakes and canals. These canoes were dugouts or plank-built, flat-bottomed, and paddle-powered, ideal for the shallow waters.\n\nThey carried food, building materials, tribute goods and market supplies. They also served as transport for soldiers or messengers. These canoes formed the backbone of the Aztec logistics and trade system across the empire’s vast network of waterways."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_TEPOZTLI_CANOE"   ;   Str = "Tepoztli Canoes are reinforced wooden canoes, each mounted with a giant tepoztli (axe) to smash apart enemy ships. These flat-bottomed, paddle-powered canoes are perfect for ramming and close combat, as they maneuver the shallow lakes and canals of central Mexico with ease."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_ARROW_CANOE"   ;   Str = "Arrow Canoes are narrow, maneuverable vessels manned by skilled warriors, equipped with ranged weapons, such as bows and slings. Fashioned from hollowed-out logs or built with planks, they have a flat bottom for stability in shallow waters and are paddle-powered by several men.\n\nArrow canoes allow quick troop movements, surprise attacks, and amphibious assaults, playing a key role in Aztec military strategy."

ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_POPOCATEPETL"   ;   Str = "Age -- 24\nHomeland -- Aztlan\nOccupation -- Aztec Warrior\n\nPopocatepetl was a fearless Aztec warrior, devoted to the beautiful princess Iztaccihuatl. Before leaving for battle, he vowed to return victorious and make her his bride. Before he could do so, false news of his death reached her, overcome with grief, she died of heartbreak.\n\nWhen Popocatepetl returned, he carried her body to the mountains and laid her upon the earth. Struck by grief he remained by her side, his torch burning through the cold nights, until his own life faded.\n\nThe gods took pity and transformed them into mountains; Iztaccihuatl, 'the Sleeping Woman', and Popocatepetl, 'the Smoking Mountain'. His fiery breath still rises as an eternal flame for the love he could never let go."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_IZTACCIHUATL"   ;   Str = "Age -- 24\nHomeland -- Aztlan\nOccupation -- Aztec Warrior\n\nIztaccihuatl was a princess of radiant beauty and gentle heart, beloved by the warrior Popocatepetl. Her father promised their union if he returned victorious from battle. But deceit shattered their fate as false messengers brought word of Popocatepetl’s death.\n\nCrushed by sorrow, Iztaccihuatl’s heart broke, and she fell into an eternal sleep. When Popocatepetl returned and found her lifeless, grief consumed him as well.\n\nThe gods, moved by their love, transformed them into mountains; Iztaccihuatl, 'the Sleeping Woman', lying in quiet grace, and Popocatepetl, 'the Smoking Mountain', keeping eternal watch by her side."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_TLACAELEL"   ;   Str = "Age -- 32\nHomeland -- Aztlan\nOccupation -- Aztec Warrior\n\nTlacaelel was a powerful and influential Aztec noble, military leader, and reformer. Though he never became emperor himself, he served as chief advisor to several Aztec rulers. Tlacaelel  helped shape the Aztec Empire’s political, military, and religious foundations.\n\nTlacaelel was a brilliant strategist who strengthened the power of the emperor, restructured the government, and elevated the sun god Huitzilopochtli to the top of the Aztec pantheon. He also promoted the Flower Wars ritual battles used to train warriors and gather captives for sacrifice, emphasizing the role of human sacrifice in sustaining the gods."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_ITZCOATL"   ;   Str = "Age -- 35\nHomeland -- Aztlan\nOccupation -- Aztec Warrior\n\nItzcoatl, whose name translates to 'Obsidian Serpent', is as sharp and dark as volcanic glass. He rose from humble beginnings to lead the Mexica people out from beneath the shadow of Tepanec domination. Under his command, the warriors of Tenochtitlan cast off their overlords and, through cunning diplomacy and iron resolve, forged a powerful alliance with Texcoco and Tlacopan. This Triple Alliance became the beating heart of what would soon be known as the Aztec Empire, a power destined to dominate the Valley of Mexico.\n\nGuided by the brilliant and ruthless counsel of his advisor Tlacaelel, Itzcoatl reshaped the very soul of his people. Together they restructured society, elevating warriors and priests as the twin pillars of the new state. In a sweeping act of political alchemy, Itzcoatl ordered the destruction of the old histories to clear the path for a new mythic vision. In their place arose a doctrine of divine purpose, the worship of Huitzilopochtli. As the god of sun and war he was made patron of the empire and represented the destiny of the Mexica.\n\nThrough fire and faith, through conquest and creation, Itzcoatl transformed a struggling island city into the nucleus of a civilization that would reshape an entire world."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_MALINALXOCHITL"   ;   Str = "Age -- 50\nHomeland -- Aztlan\nOccupation -- Goddess of snakes, scorpions and black magic\n\nMalinalxochitl, whose name translates to 'the Grass Flower', was no ordinary woman. She was a sorceress of immense power, a goddess woven from serpents and shadow. Feared and revered in equal measure, she ruled over the realms of magic, snakes, and night, her presence a whisper in the dark winds of the desert. Those who spoke her name did so with both awe and caution, for she was said to weave spells as easily as others might breathe.\n\nSister to the mighty war god Huitzilopochtli, Malinalxochitl once marched beside him during the Mexica migration, leading her own devoted followers. Yet even divine kinship could not shield her from betrayal. Opposed to her atrocities and wary of her sorcery, her brother abandoned her in her sleep. Leaving Malinalxochitl, behind in the desert sands.\n\nFrom that moment, Malinalxochitl became a figure of both vengeance and mystery. A goddess of the forsaken, a protector of those who walk the night’s edge. She embodies the duality at the heart of Aztec myth, creation and destruction, beauty and danger. Light and the serpentine darkness beneath it."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_COPIL"   ;   Str = "Age -- 28\nHomeland -- Aztlan\nOccupation – Aztec Warrior \n\nCopil, son of Malinalxochitl and nephew of Huitzilopochtli, stands in legend as a figure of defiance. A spirit who dared to resist the destiny of the Mexica. Born of divine blood yet bound by mortal conviction, Copil opposed his uncle’s growing power and the relentless march of the Mexica across the sacred lands of Anahuac. To him, their ambition was a wound upon the earth, and he sought to turn them back with word and will alike.\n\nBut defiance against a god is a perilous path. When Copil raised his voice in insult and rebellion, Huitzilopochtli's wrath descended upon him like a storm of obsidian blades. The god struck him down, tearing from his chest the still-beating heart that burned with pride and resistance. That heart was cast into the soil of Lake Texcoco, where it took root in divine memory. From it sprang the nopal, the prickly pear cactus - its red fruit like drops of Copil's immortal blood.\n\nIn time, the wandering Mexica found that sacred sign, an eagle perched upon the cactus devouring a serpent. There, upon the place born from Copil’s sacrifice, they built their destined city, Tenochtitlan. Thus, the heart of their greatest foe became the heart of their empire, and resistance itself was woven into the foundation of their world."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_MICTLANTECUHTLI"   ;   Str = "Age -- Ageless\nHomeland -- Mictlan\nOccupation -- God of the dead and the Underworld\n\nMictlantecuhtli, the Lord of Mictlan, reigns over the shadowed kingdom beneath the earth, the silent realm where all mortal journeys end. Pale as moonlight on bone, he is the god of death and the underworld, a skeletal sovereign draped in the trappings of decay and eternity. His fleshless grin and hollow eyes are not merely symbols of terror but of truth, that all life is bound to the endless cycle of death and rebirth.\n\nBeside him rules his consort, Mictecacihuatl, the Lady of the Dead. Together they preside over Mictlan, the deepest of the nine underworlds, where the souls of those who die natural deaths travel through trials of wind, stone, and darkness before finding rest.\n\nMictlantecuhtli’s domain is not one of cruelty, but of inevitable return. A place where the essence of the living is stripped bare, reduced to bone and spirit, and folded back into the vast silence from which all things arise. His emblems of skulls, bones, and owls are reminders that death is not the end, but the other face of life, grim, sacred, and eternal."

ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_QUETZALCOATL"   ;   Str = "Age -- Ageless\nHomeland -- Aztlan\nOccupation -- God of Wind, Arts, and Wisdom\n\nQuetzalcoatl, the Feathered Serpent, soars between sky and earth as one of the most luminous deities. Covered in brilliant, iridescent feathers, he coils and winds like the living wind, a bridge between the mortal world and the divine. He is the god of wind, wisdom, learning, culture, and creation, whose breath shaped mountains, rivers, and the very hearts of humankind.\n\nAs a creator, Quetzalcoatl molded the first humans from sacred elements and infused the world with knowledge and fertility. He bestowed the gifts of agriculture, the calendar, writing, and the arts, guiding his people toward civilization and order. His luminous presence is tied to the morning star, Venus, heralding new beginnings and illuminating the path of life with clarity and foresight.\n\nBeloved as a civilizing force, Quetzalcoatl embodies both wisdom and compassion, a god who champions the mind and the spirit. Ancient prophecies foretold his return, a belief that lingered for generations and echoed with profound significance when distant strangers arrived from across the sea. His legacy endures, a radiant serpent weaving through time, a reminder of humanity's potential for creation, learning, and transcendence."

ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_HUITZILOPOCHTLI"   ;   Str = "Age -- Ageless\nHomeland -- Aztlan\nOccupation -- Lord of War and the Sun\n\nHuitzilopochtli, the Hummingbird of the South, blazes across the heavens as the god of the sun and war, the eternal guardian and patron of the Aztec people. His wings shimmer like the first rays of dawn, carrying the warmth and fury of the sun as he battles the encroaching darkness each night. From the moment of their wandering, he guided the Mexica across the lands, leading them to the sacred site where they would build Tenochtitlan, the heart of their destined empire.\n\nAs the solar warrior, Huitzilopochtli demanded strength through sacrifice, a fiery exchange that empowered him to rise each morning and continue the relentless struggle against the forces of night. His presence was not merely divine but the embodiment of military power, unity, and national identity, inspiring the Aztec warriors to conquer, endure, and thrive.\n\nIn his duality, he was both nurturing and fearsome, the giver of light and life, yet the relentless destroyer of enemies. To honor him was to affirm the survival, ambition, and destiny of the Aztec people, for Huitzilopochtli's victory over darkness was inseparable from their own triumphs in war, culture, and empire."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_UNITS_TEZCATLIPOCA"   ;   Str = "Age -- Ageless\nHomeland -- Aztlan\nOccupation -- God of Obsidian, Darkness, and Sorcery\n\nTezcatlipoca, the Smoking Mirror, is one of the most enigmatic and formidable gods in Aztec mythology. Cloaked in shadow and mystery, he rules over night, sorcery, the sky, and the threads of fate. His obsidian mirror reflects the truth and illusions of the world, revealing the hidden desires and fears of gods and mortals alike.\n\nA god of chaos and transformation, Tezcatlipoca embodies the forces that shape (and sometimes shatter) creation. He is a master of conflict, cunning, and power; his influence twisting destinies and challenging even the most revered deities, including Quetzalcoatl, whom he often opposed. In the Aztec cosmology, he played a central role in the creation and destruction of successive worlds, reminding all that existence is fragile and ever-changing.\n\nUnpredictable, and awe-inspiring, Tezcatlipoca was both feared and venerated. He demanded respect, not through generosity, but through the recognition of his power to govern fortune, misfortune, and the invisible forces that shape life itself. In his gaze, nothing is permanent - only the eternal dance of shadow and revelation endures."

They are elusive and shy, often perching quietly in the forest canopy."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_EMB_VULTURE"   ;   Str = "Scientific name - Cathartes aura\nSize - 1.8 to 5.1 lbs\nDiet - Carrion feeder\n\nThe turkey vulture is a large, soaring bird of prey known for its bald red head, dark body, and broad wings. Found throughout the Americas, it is highly recognizable in flight, gliding with its wings held in a shallow V shape and teetering from side to side. Turkey vultures are scavengers, playing a vital ecological role by cleaning up animal carcasses. Despite their ominous appearance, they are non-aggressive and rely heavily on their strong sense of smell to locate food; a rare trait among birds."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_EMB_TOUCAN"   ;   Str = "Scientific name - Ramphastos toco \nDiet - Omnivore\n\nThe toucan is a brightly colored, tropical bird best known for its large, colorful bill, which can be nearly as long as its body. Native to Central and South America, toucans inhabit rainforests and are often seen in the canopy. Their oversized bill, though lightweight, helps them reach fruit on distant branches, regulate body temperature, and engage in social interactions. Toucans are social, often living in small flocks, and their loud, croaking calls can be heard echoing through the forest."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_EMB_TAPIR"   ;   Str = "Scientific name - Tapirus terrestris \nSize - 330 to 700 lbs \nDiet - Herbivore\n\nA tapir is a large, herbivorous mammal with a distinctive, short, trunk-like snout used for grabbing leaves and fruit. Resembling a mix between a pig and an anteater, tapirs are actually most closely related to horses and rhinoceroses. They inhabit forests and grasslands in regions including; Central America, South America and Southeast Asia, depending on the species. Tapirs are generally shy, solitary, and excellent swimmers, often found near water where they cool off and escape predators."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_EMB_JAGUAR"   ;   Str = "Scientific name - Panthera onca\nSize - 100 to 265 lbs\nDiet - Carnivore\n\nThe jaguar is a powerful, solitary big cat, native to the Americas, primarily found in rainforests, wetlands, and grasslands of Central and South America. Known for its muscular build, spotted golden coat, and strong jaws, the jaguar is the largest cat in the Americas and the third-largest in the world, after tigers and lions. Unlike most big cats, jaguars are strong swimmers and often hunt in and around water. They play a vital role as apex predators, maintaining the balance of their ecosystem."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_EMB_JAGUAR_BLACK"   ;   Str = "Scientific name - Panthera onca\nSize - 100 to 265 lbs\nDiet - Carnivore\n\nThe jaguar is a powerful, solitary big cat, native to the Americas, primarily found in rainforests, wetlands, and grasslands of Central and South America. Known for its muscular build, spotted golden coat, and strong jaws, the jaguar is the largest cat in the Americas and the third-largest in the world, after tigers and lions. Unlike most big cats, jaguars are strong swimmers and often hunt in and around water. They play a vital role as apex predators, maintaining the balance of their ecosystem."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_EMB_OCELOT"   ;   Str = "Scientific name - Leopardus pardalis\nSize - 18 to 40 lbs\nDiet - Carnivore\n\nThe ocelot is a sleek, medium-sized wild cat native to the forests, grasslands, and marshes across many regions including; Central America, South America and Southern United States. It has a striking coat with golden-yellow fur covered in black rosettes and spots, providing excellent camouflage. Ocelots are solitary, nocturnal, and territorial hunters. Agile climbers and swimmers, they are secretive and elusive, preferring dense vegetation. Though smaller than other wild cats, they are fierce predators with sharp senses and stealthy movements."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_EMB_MACAW"   ;   Str = "Scientific name - Ara macao – Scarlet Macaw \nSize - 2 to 3.3 lbs\nDiet - Herbivore\n\nMacaws are large, colorful parrots native to the rainforests of Central and South America. Known for their striking plumage; often bright red, blue, yellow, and green, and their strong, curved beaks, macaws are highly intelligent and social birds. They live in flocks, communicate with loud calls, and are excellent flyers. Macaws are also popular in aviculture due to their playful nature and ability to mimic sounds. In the wild, they play a vital role in seed dispersal, helping maintain healthy forest ecosystems."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_EMB_DOLPHIN"   ;   Str = "Scientific name - Delphinus delphis \nSize - 110 to 1,430 lbs \nDiet - Carnivore\n\nDolphins are highly intelligent, social marine mammals known for their streamlined bodies, playful behavior, and complex communication skills. They belong to the family of toothed whales and are found worldwide in oceans and some freshwater rivers. Dolphins use echolocation to navigate and hunt, and they often live in groups called pods. Known for their agility and speed, dolphins are beloved for their friendly interactions with humans and other marine animals."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_EMB_GIANT_OTTER"   ;   Str = "Scientific name - Pteronura brasiliensis \nSize - 48 to 70 lbs \nDiet - Carnivore\n\nThe giant otter is a large, social, and highly vocal freshwater mammal native to the rivers and lakes of the Amazon Basin in South America. Known for its sleek, muscular body and distinctive dark brown fur with pale throat markings, the giant otter is an excellent swimmer and hunter. They live in family groups, often called “rafts,” and are territorial animals. Giant otters are playful but fierce when defending their territory. They are considered endangered due to habitat loss and hunting."

Native to the tropical wetlands and rainforests of South America, they are non-venomous constrictors that kill prey by coiling around and suffocating it. Anacondas are excellent swimmers, often spending much of their time in rivers and swamps. Despite their intimidating size, they are generally shy and avoid humans."

They are known for their soft, luxurious fleece, which is highly valued in the textile industry. Smaller than llamas, alpacas are social herd animals. They graze on grasses and adapt well to harsh mountain climates, making them well-suited to life in rugged environments."

Domesticated turkeys, bred from wild ancestors, are widely raised for their meat. Known for their distinctive fan-shaped tails and gobbling calls, turkeys are social animals that often travel in groups called flocks. Their adaptability has allowed them to thrive in both wild and agricultural environments."

ID = "STR_HISTORY_EMB_KAPOK"   ;   Str = "The Kapok tree is a tall tropical tree known for its impressive height and lightweight, fluffy seed fibers. It grows in rainforests, primarily in Central and South America, West Africa, and Southeast Asia. The tree produces large, colorful flowers and seed pods filled with a cotton-like fiber called kapok, which is buoyant and water-resistant. This fiber has been traditionally used for stuffing pillows, mattresses, and life jackets. The Kapok tree also plays an important ecological role by providing habitat and food for various animals."

It is characterized by its single, straight trunk topped with a crown of large, fan-shaped leaves. These palms are often used as ornamental trees in landscaping due to their graceful appearance and ability to thrive in warm, dry climates. They can tolerate drought and poor soil, making them popular in urban environments."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_EMB_PALO_VERDE"   ;   Str = "The Palo Verde is a drought-tolerant tree native to the southwestern United States and northern Mexico. It translates to 'green stick' in Spanish, referring to its green bark, which can perform photosynthesis even when the tree has shed its leaves during dry periods. Palo Verde trees have delicate, small leaves and produce bright yellow flowers in the spring, which attract pollinators like bees and hummingbirds. They are well-adapted to arid environments and are commonly used in desert landscaping."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_EMB_YUUCA"   ;   Str = "Yucca is a genus of perennial plants native to the arid regions of the Americas (especially the southwestern United States and Mexico). They are known for their tough, sword-shaped leaves and tall flower spikes that produce clusters of white or cream-colored bell-shaped flowers. Yuccas are well-adapted to dry, desert environments and are often used in landscaping for their dramatic, architectural appearance. Many species have cultural and practical uses, such as for fiber, soap, and food."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_EMB_CACTUS"   ;   Str = "Cacti are a diverse group of succulent plants native primarily to the Americas, especially arid and semi-arid regions like deserts. They are well-known for their thick, fleshy stems that store water, allowing them to survive in harsh, dry environments. Most cacti have spines instead of leaves, which help reduce water loss and protect them from herbivores. They produce beautiful flowers that bloom seasonally and are often pollinated by birds, bats, or insects. Cacti vary greatly in size and shape, from small ground-hugging plants to tall, tree-like forms."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_EMB_NOLINA_PARRYI"   ;   Str = "Nolina parryi, commonly known as 'Parry’s beargrass' or 'Parry nolina', is a drought-tolerant, perennial plant native to southern California and northern Baja California. It features long, narrow, tough leaves that grow in dense rosettes, often with serrated edges. The plant produces tall flowering stalks with clusters of small white flowers. Nolina parryi thrives in rocky, dry habitats like chaparral and desert slopes, and it’s well-adapted to survive in harsh, arid conditions."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_EMB_AGAVE"   ;   Str = "Agave plants are a striking genus of succulent monocots native to the arid and semi-arid regions of the Americas (particularly Mexico and the southwestern United States). Renowned for their resilience and unique beauty, agaves thrive where few other plants can, embodying the spirit of endurance and adaptation in harsh desert landscapes.\n\nThese plants typically grow in large rosettes of thick, fleshy leaves, often tipped with sharp spines and edged with serrations that serve both as protection and a hallmark of their distinctive form. Their xerophytic nature allows them to store water efficiently, making them masters of survival in environments of intense heat and scarcity.\n\nBeyond their ecological role, agaves hold deep cultural and economic significance. Ancient Mesoamerican civilizations revered them as sacred plants, sources of food, fiber, medicine, and the fermented beverage pulque. Today, they remain vital for producing agave syrup and the celebrated spirits mezcal and tequila, linking the plant’s ancient roots to modern tradition.\n\nThe agave, with its dramatic form and enduring utility, stands as both a symbol of the desert's stark beauty and a testament to the intricate relationship between nature and culture across the Americas."
// Buildings
ID = "STR_HISTORY_BLD_CALPULLI"   ;   Str = "In pre-Columbian Aztec society, the calpulli translated to 'large houses', served as foundational community units within Nahua city-states, or altepetls. Each calpulli was composed of families or kin-based groups who shared land, labor, and responsibility. Together, they organized the cultivation of crops, managed food production, and oversaw the creation of goods and tribute owed to the state. Functioning as both a social and economic hub, the calpulli was the heart of everyday life, where resources such as food, wood, and precious materials were gathered, produced, and distributed for the well-being of the larger community."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_BLD_CITADEL_CENTER"   ;   Str = "When the Town Center is forged into a Citadel, it becomes an unyielding fortress, a bastion of strength against the fiercest onslaughts. This mighty stronghold stands firm against waves of enemy soldiers, legendary myth units, and devastating god powers. Though its walls can be repaired after battle scars, once the Citadel falls completely, it cannot rise again, making its defense the cornerstone of your survival and victory."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_BLD_GREAT_TEMPLE"   ;   Str = "Rising like a sacred mountain from the heart of the city, the Great Temple is the divine nexus where gods and mortals meet. Crafted by the hands of master builders and consecrated by ancient priests, this towering pyramid is both a place of worship and a fortress of spiritual power. Its steps echo with the chants of ceremonies honoring the sun, rain, and war gods. It invokes the blessings and protection of the gods, promising victory.\n\nWithin its shadow, warriors find courage, and priests channel the divine will to sway the fate of kingdoms. The Great Temple's walls are adorned with intricate carvings telling tales of creation, sacrifice, and cosmic balance, reminding all who enter of the eternal dance between light and darkness.\n\nIt is said that the Great Temple holds the heartbeat of the Aztec people. A sacred beacon that, while standing tall and unyielding, binds the mortal world with the gods’ eternal watch."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_BLD_TZOMPANTLI_TOWER"   ;   Str = "Towers initially cannot attack units directly beneath them, but researching Boiling Oil will permit them to do so. Ranged attacks have difficulty hitting fast-moving targets, such as cavalry. Research Crenellations to improve the chance to hit such units.\n\nA tzompantli or skull rack was a type of wooden rack or palisade documented in several Mesoamerican civilizations. It was used for the public display of human skulls, typically those of war captives or other sacrificial victims. It is a scaffold-like construction of poles on which heads and skulls were placed."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_BLD_WAR_HUT"   ;   Str = "In Aztec society, membership in the calpulli established each individual's religious and secular schooling, as well as how they would be trained in warfare.\n\nThe War Hut draws inspiration from the historical telpochcalli (“houses of youth”), which played an important role in preparing its students for military life. These schools acted as both training grounds and living spaces, where young men built strength through tough physical exercises and learned how to use different weapons. Their instruction came from veteran warriors, who passed on practical combat skills and discipline gained from real battlefield experience."

In the Noble’s Hut young warriors are groomed for leadership and trained in the elite ways of religion, governance and warfare."

ID = "STR_HISTORY_BLD_SMOKE_TRAP" ; Str = "Smoke Traps are concealed devices that can be set to trigger automatically or lie in wait for the perfect moment. When activated, they release a thick cloud that blinds nearby enemies, slows their movement, and obscures the battlefield, making it difficult for affected foes to track or engage your units. Carefully placed, these traps can disrupt advances and turn the tide of an encounter.\n\nThe use of smoke and concealment has long been a tool of cunning warfare, allowing warriors to confuse, disorient, and outmaneuver their enemies. Whether drawn from ritual practice or battlefield ingenuity, shrouding one’s movements in haze creates an unseen advantage. Smoke Traps embody this strategy, turning vision into vulnerability and chaos into opportunity."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_BLD_SPIKE_TRAP" ; Str = "Spike Traps are hidden hazards that can be set to trigger automatically or held in reserve for precise timing. When activated, they spring forth with deadly force, briefly stunning nearby enemies and inflicting grievous wounds over time. Well-placed traps can halt an advance in its tracks, punishing any who venture too close.\n\nAcross cultures and battlefields, concealed spikes have been used to maim, deter, and control the flow of combat. From simple pitfalls to ingeniously crafted mechanisms, they turn the ground itself into a weapon. Spike Traps embody this ruthless ingenuity, striking without warning and leaving enemies ensnared in pain and chaos."
// Locations
ID = "STR_HISTORY_LOCATION_AZTLAN"   ;   Str = "Aztlan, the legendary ancestral homeland of the Aztec (Mexica) people, is a place shrouded in myth and memory. Aztlan was a shimmering land said to lie far to the north, where the waters glisten like polished jade and white herons glide over tranquil lakes. It was a realm of beauty and abundance, a paradise of fertile fields and sacred mountains. The ancestors of the Mexica lived in harmony before destiny called them southward.\n\nFrom this radiant homeland, the Mexica began their long and arduous migration, guided by divine omens and the will of their god Huitzilopochtli. A journey that would lead them through trials, deserts, and revelations until they founded the mighty city of Tenochtitlán, the heart of an empire.\n\nThe name Aztlan, translated as 'Place of Whiteness' or 'Place of the Herons' in Nahuatl, evokes both purity and mystery. It is a symbol of origin, identity, and the eternal search for a promised land."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_LOCATION_TENOCHTITLAN"   ;   Str = "Tenochtitlán, also known as Mexico-Tenochtitlán, was the dazzling heart of the Aztec world. It is defined by a grand altepetl rising from the shimmering waters of Lake Texcoco, where temples, palaces, and canals intertwined in a masterpiece of engineering and sacred design. Founded, according to tradition, on March 13th 1325, the city’s origins are veiled in legend. The Mexica, following a divine vision, saw an eagle perched upon a cactus devouring a serpent and treated it as a sign from their god Huitzilopochtli that this was the land destined for greatness.\n\nBuilt upon an island in the Valley of Mexico, Tenochtitlán grew into a vast metropolis of glittering causeways, floating gardens (chinampas), and bustling marketplaces that awed all who entered its gates. By the 15th century, it stood as the proud capital of the Aztec Empire, a city of power, art, and devotion, where the sacred and the earthly intertwined.\n\nBut its glory was not eternal. In 1521, after fierce battles and betrayal, Tenochtitlán fell to the combined forces of the Spanish conquistadors and their Tlaxcaltec allies. Its temples burned and its canals silenced. Despite its fall, the spirit of Tenochtitlán endures, living on in the heart of modern Mexico City, built upon its ancient foundations."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_LOCATION_MICTLAN"   ;   Str = "Mictlan, the shadowed underworld of Aztec mythology, is a vast and somber realm lying deep beneath the earth. Mictlan is a place where the souls of most mortals journey after death. Neither a place of torment nor reward, Mictlan is instead a land of silence, mystery, and transformation. The departed must endure a long and perilous passage through nine distinct levels before finding eternal rest.\n\nThe journey through Mictlan is said to takes years, each level testing the spirit’s endurance and resolve. Along the way they will cross raging rivers, pass between crashing mountains, and brave winds filled with obsidian knives. Yet the dead do not travel alone, they are guided by Xolotl, the loyal and watchful psychopomp, a divine dog-headed deity who leads them safely through the trials of the underworld.\n\nAt the deepest level of Mictlan resides Mictlantecuhtli and Mictecacihuatl, the Lord and Lady of Death, enthroned amid bones and shadows, guardians of the realm’s stillness. Mictlan also plays a vital role in the Aztec creation myth, for it is from its silent depths that the gods once retrieved the bones of past worlds; the sacred remains from which humanity itself was reborn.\n\nIn Mictlan, death is not an end, but a return, a long descent into the heart of the earth, where memory and divinity intertwine."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_LOCATION_HEL"   ;   Str = "Hel is the cold, mist-shrouded realm of the dead in Norse mythology. It is a somber afterlife ruled by the enigmatic and formidable goddess Hel, daughter of Loki and the giantess Angrboða. Hidden far beneath the roots of Yggdrasil, the great World Tree, Hel is a place where those who did not die in battle (the old, the sick, and the ordinary) find their rest after death.\n\nUnlike the fiery torments of later mythologies, Hel is a realm of quiet and gloom, neither wholly cruel nor kind. Its halls, built from shadows and frost, are said to be immense and cold, filled with the whispers of countless souls. The river Gjöll, icy and glittering like glass, flows through its domain, crossed by the bridge Gjallarbrú, which guards the boundary between the living and the dead.\n\nHel herself is a striking figure, half beautiful woman, half corpse, signifying the duality of life and decay. Stern but just, she governs her realm with unyielding order, ensuring that all who enter find their rightful place in the endless twilight of her kingdom."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_LOCATION_HADES"   ;   Str = "Hades, the Underworld, is the shadowed realm of the dead. It sits as one of the three great divisions of the cosmos, alongside the heavens and the sea. Hidden deep beneath the earth, it is a vast and mysterious domain ruled by Hades, the stern and just god of the dead. He ensures that all souls find their proper place beyond the veil of life.\n\nThe journey to Hades begins at the banks of the dark River Styx, where the ferryman Charon carries the departed across its murky waters. This trip is reserved for those who bear a coin for passage. Within its depths, lie many rivers: the fiery Phlegethon, the sorrowful Acheron, the forgetful Lethe, and the unbreakable Cocytus. Beyond them stretch the shadowy fields where souls wander, recalling faint echoes of their mortal lives.\n\nYet the Underworld is not merely a place of gloom. Within it are realms of judgment and reward: The Elysian Fields, where heroes and the virtuous rest in eternal peace, and Tartarus, the deep abyss where the wicked face divine retribution. Hades is thus a place of balance, reflection, and destiny. It is the silent heart of the Greek cosmos, where every life’s story finds its final chapter."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_LOCATION_PLENTY_VAULT"   ;   Str = "The Plenty Vault is a mystical structure, a gleaming symbol of divine abundance and favor. Created when the power of Plenty is invoked, the Vault radiates prosperity. Its sacred aura steadily generates resources for its owner as if the gods themselves were filling mortal coffers.\n\nUnlike ordinary buildings, the Plenty Vault is indestructible, a timeless gift of the divine that cannot be shattered by fire or siege. Yet its blessings are not bound forever, should the guardians of the Vault fall or stray too far, its bounty may change hands. Any player, enemy or not, can claim its wealth by seizing control of the area around it.\n\nMajestic and enduring, the Plenty Vault stands as both a treasure and a temptation. It serves as a divine prize that can turn the tide of battle, rewarding those clever or bold enough to claim its endless riches."
// Gameplay Concepts
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TONALLI"   ;   Str = "In Mesoamerican belief (particularly among the ancient Nahua), tonalli represented a vital life force; an energy that infused the body with warmth, strength, and vitality. Closely linked to the sun’s heat and to one’s personal destiny or day sign, tonalli was thought to reside in the hair and the crown of the head. It was this essence that granted a person their vigor, growth, and spirit.\n\nEnemies defeated by the Aztecs drop tonalli orbs, which are collected by nearby Warrior Priests and religious buildings (Tzompantlis, Temples and Great Temples). In return, the gods reward the Aztecs with Favor. \n\nTonalli is a vital source Favor for the Aztecs, so be sure to collect it."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_STEALTH"   ;   Str = "Faced with superior military technology during the Spanish invasion, the Aztecs employed guerrilla warfare tactics, setting up traps and hiding in wait for their stronger adversaries.\n\nA number of Aztec units possess the Foliage Stealth ability, making them invisible to their enemies, while in range of the tree line. Whilst these units are capable fighters at any time, stealth affords the element of surprise.\n\nUnits move in and out of stealth automatically, remaining visible to the owner as a silhouette. They are only revealed by enemies at close range, although scout-type units and Towers reveal them from afar.\n\nWhile stealth units are selected, the cursor icon indicates a Move destination providing stealth cover.\n\nStealth units include the Quimichin Spy, Ocelotl Warrior, Jaguar Rider and Ahuizotl, along with several campaign heroes."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TRAPS_NAME"   ;   Str = "Traps"
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TRAPS"   ;   Str = "Quimichin Spies and War Hut units can build two main trap types; Smoke Traps and Spike Traps.\n\nSmoke Traps reduce enemies' line of sight and movement speed, making them vulnerable to ambushes. They can be upgraded to Chilli Bomb Traps, increasing each of their base effects.\n\nSpike Traps impale enemies who walk over them, making them bleed, losing health over time. Spike Traps can be upgraded to Obsidian Spike Traps, increasing their damage.\n\nTraps remain hidden to enemy units until they come very close, triggering when they walk over them. If an enemy attacks a trap before it triggers, it becomes disarmed."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_DEVOTION"   ;   Str = "The Aztec universe is unstable and continually threatened by destruction. Devotion (sacrifice) is key to balancing and preserving the universe, as the devotee gives up their life force to sustain the gods.\n\nDevotion is a supplementary mechanic to tonalli gathering. Devoting a Settler at the Temple provides a sum of Favor, and increases Favor trickle rate. However, your Settler is lost to the gods, never to return.\n\nDevotion’s Favor reward offers a spike in short term power, at the cost of long term resource gathering. Used wisely however, the myth units, technologies and god powers you buy with that Favor could provide a pivotal edge over your opponent."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_CEREMONIES_NAME"   ;   Str = "Great Temple Ceremonies"
ID = "STR_HISTORY_CEREMONIES"   ;   Str = "The Aztec religion pervaded all aspects of the peoples' lives. It’s various rituals and ceremonies were closely bound to the calendar. Some of these rituals called for certain types of offerings, including crops, animals and human lives.\n\nThe Great Temple has 3 ceremonies, each offering unique rewards, whilst requiring the Devotion (sacrifice) of different unit types:\n\nNew Fire Ceremony - Earn bonus Favor from Devotion for 60 sec. (Cost: 3 Livestock)\n\nCosmic Guard - 6 myth unit spawn at your Great Temple (3 from Age IV and 3 from Age III). (Cost: 15 Human Soldiers)\n\nArrival of the Gods - Your major god manifests to fight on your behalf; a stronger version of the Teixiptla, with increased lifespan and combat stats. (Cost: 6 Warrior Priests)"
ID = "STR_HISTORY_YOLIKNIN_NAME"   ;   Str = "Yoliknin"
ID = "STR_HISTORY_YOLIKNIN"   ;   Str = "Whilst concealing his identity during the Obsidian Mirror campaign, Quetzalcoatl introduces himself to Popocatepetl as Yoliknin.\n\nYoliknin is a Nahuatl word deriving from Yollotl (heart) and Ikniu (friend). It can be translated as ‘brother or friend of the heart’.\n\nIn Aztec (Mexica) culture, the root term icniuhtli carried deep social and emotional meaningm, more than just friendship, it implied a bond of loyalty, trust, and shared experience. It could refer to a close ally in daily life, a companion in battle, or even a trusted confidant in ritual or political affairs.\n\nIn poetic or ceremonial contexts, calling someone icniuhtli was an expression of deep respect and affection. This was a recognition of kinship forged not by blood, but by mutual honor and connection." 
// Technologies
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_WINGS_OF_THE_SOUTH"   ;   Str = "Huitzilopochtli’s name literally translates to “Hummingbird on the Left,” a reference to the southern direction in Aztec cartographic tradition. The Mexica believed that warriors who fell in glory ascended to the heavens to escort the sun on its daily journey, only to be reincarnated as hummingbirds after four years of divine service. By honoring the “Hummingbird of the South”, the empire’s soldiers are imbued with this tireless, frantic energy, marching without fear in the knowledge that death is merely a transformation."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_TEPEYOLLOTLS_REACH"   ;   Str = "The jaguar, or ocelotl, was revered by the Aztecs as the ultimate symbol of the warrior elite. The omnipotent god Tezcatlipoca often manifested as Tepeyollotl 'Heart of the Mountain', a fearsome jaguar deity famously depicted in ancient codices leaping valiantly towards the sun. Warriors who don the jaguar skin channel this specific divine aspect, striking their prey with the sudden, violent grace of the mountain god."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_FEAST_OF_TLAXOCHIMACO"   ;   Str = "It was the Feathered Serpent, Quetzalcoatl, who first ventured into the mythical Mountain of Sustenance (Tonacatépetl) to gift maize to a starving humanity. Unable to unlock the food stores hidden deep inside the mountain, he called upon the lame god Nanahuatzin, who broke it open with thunderbolts.\n\nTo repay this eternal debt without shedding human blood, the Aztecs offer their livestock during a frantic, sleepless vigil. Honored by these communal sacrifices, the great civilizer breathes a second wind into his people, granting them supernatural swiftness in their daily labors."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_OMETOCHTLIS_REVELRY"   ;   Str = "Ometochtli, the 'Two Rabbit' deity, presided over the four hundred unpredictable gods of pulque. When spirits or mortals succumbed to their intoxicating influence, they were thrown into a violent, insatiable frenzy.\n\nIn their unbridled hunger, the Centzon Tōtōchtin embody both the exuberance and danger of the god’s revelry, boisterously devouring anything in their path."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_OCPATLI_INFUSIONS"   ;   Str = "Patecatl discovered the magical ocpatli roots, which were used to ferment a potent, mind-altering elixir known as 'obsidian medicine'. Administered before battle or ritual sacrifice, this sacred infusion stripped away all earthly fears and dulled physical pain.\n\nImbibing Ocpatli’s Infusion fills warriors with a divine, reckless courage, allowing them to endure and recover from the most grievous of wounds."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_MAGUEY_CULTIVATION"   ;   Str = "The maguey was indispensable to Mesoamerican life, providing a spring of resources from a single crop. Its roasted roots offered rich sustenance, while its sweet sap was fermented into potent tonics. Cultivating this plant brought abundance of food and materials."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_OMEN_OF_MALINALCO"   ;   Str = "Encountering a two-headed maquizcoatl is a dark omen of inescapable doom. Having being abandoned in the dead of night by her brother Huitzilopochtli, the sorceress Malinalxochitl infuses her lingering resentment into the venomous creatures. Even when struck down, the beasts fulfill their prophecy by erupting in a caustic vapor. This toxic cloud eats away at their killers' defenses, ensuring the witch's enemies meet a bitter end."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_STING_OF_YAPPAN"   ;   Str = "Yappan was a mortal ascetic who took a strict vow of chastity, only to be seduced by the fertility goddess Xochiquetzal. As punishment for his broken oath, Yappan was cursed to crawl the earth as the first scorpion. The sorceress Malinalxochitl channels the disgraced man's agonizing spite into her followers' armaments. Those pierced by these blades suffer a burning, lingering toxin, sharing in Yappan's eternal punishment."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_TOLOACHE_TRANCE"   ;   Str = "Ancient Mesoamericans respected the Toloache plant for its ability to induce deep, shamanic trances and erratic hallucinations. Because of its dark associations, the sorceress Malinalxochitl wields its potent sap as a battlefield curse. When her followers strike an enemy with weapons laced in this transdermal narcotic, the victim's nervous system is instantly numbed. Trapped in a sluggish, waking nightmare, heavily afflicted foes can barely lift their weapons to defend themselves."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_OLD_COYOTES_SPIRIT"   ;   Str = "Known in Nahuatl lore as the 'owners of dangerous places', Chaneques are fiercely territorial spirits who protect the forests from trespassers. Through the blessing of the trickster god Huehuecoyotl, these shape-shifting guardians become even more unpredictable and resilient. Those who believe they have vanquished these sprites often find themselves suddenly ensnared, paying a sudden and unseen toll to the natural world."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_CUICACALLI_TRAINING"   ;   Str = "In the Cuicacalli, or House of Song, Aztec youths receive rigorous instruction in ritual dance and music, that directly conditions them for the agility needed in combat."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_TEPONAZTLI_DRUMS"   ;   Str = "Drums were an essential tactical tool for maintaining momentum and orchestrating troop movements. The teponaztli was specifically utilized as a wooden instrument to sound battle signals. During combat, captains wore small drums harnessed to their backs, which they beat to issue orders and precisely direct the movements of their squadrons across the battlefield."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_NAHUALLATOLLI"   ;   Str = "In the Aztec world view, “Nahuallatolli” literally translates to “disguised language”. Far from being a mundane dialect, it was an esoteric, highly specialized language of sorcery used for charms and spells, particularly in the context of hunting and trapping. When setting out into the wilderness, hunters and sorcerers would use Nahuallatolli in specific rituals to assert control over natural forces."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_SERPENT_SKIRT"   ;   Str = "Coatlicue, the great Aztec Earth Mother, was famously depicted wearing a terrifying skirt of writhing snakes. As a goddess who embodied both the generative womb and the devouring grave, she held absolute, dualistic power over life, death, and the soil itself.\n\nWhen her worshippers' structures are reduced to rubble, the serpents of Coatlicue’s divine skirt rise fiercely from the dirt to defend the ruins."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_COATEPEC_SHRINES"   ;   Str = "In Aztec legend, Serpent Mountain, or Coatepec, was the sacred home of the earth goddess Coatlicue. When her four hundred children besieged the mountain to slay her, the war god Huitzilopochtli miraculously burst from her womb fully armed to defend the peak. This myth of unyielding survival was so fundamental to the Aztecs that the great Templo Mayor in Tenochtitlán was constructed as a literal, symbolic replica of Coatepec."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_STRING_OF_HEARTS"   ;   Str = "In the Aztec worldview, humanity owed an eternal blood debt to the gods, and warfare was the primary means of harvesting captives to pay it.\n\nCoatlicue’s terrifying necklace of dismembered hands and hearts serves as an emblem of this divine economy, filling the hands of her warriors with dark power to crush through the strongholds of their enemies."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_CIPACTLIS_SCALES"   ;   Str = "In Aztec cosmology, the earth was forged from the dismembered body of Cipactli, a ravenous, primeval sea monster. Defeating this colossal aquatic beast required the combined might of the creator gods, who used its indestructible, spiny carapace to form the mountain ridges of the new world."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_CENTZON_HUITZNAHUA"   ;   Str = "The Centzon Huitznahua were the four hundred gods of the southern stars, an uncountable celestial swarm led by the moon goddess Coyolxauhqui.\n\nCoyolxauhqui imbues her Tzitztimimeh with the wrath and brilliance of the Centzon Huitznahua, turning them into Stardemon Tzitzimimeh."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_COYOLXAUHQUI_STONE"   ;   Str = "The Great Temples of the Aztecs were architectural reconstructions of the sacred mountain Coatepec, the mythic site where the moon goddess Coyolxauhqui was defeated by the sun. By placing a massive monolith of her dismembered body at the temple's base, priests recreated and channelled this cosmic victory."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_TECCIZTECATLS_PENANCE"   ;   Str = "When the Aztec gods gathered to create the Fifth Sun, they required a sacrifice. Tecciztécatl, the wealthy and proud 'Lord of the Snails', eagerly volunteered. He performed his pre-sacrifice penance with unparalleled extravagance, offering gold, jade, and quetzal feathers. However, when it was time to leap into the blazing sacrificial hearth, his courage failed, and he retreated four times in fear.\n\nInstead, a humble, diseased god named Nanahuatzin bravely threw himself into the fire, rising as the brilliant new sun. Shamed, Tecciztécatl finally leapt into the dying embers. Because the heavens could not have two suns, the gods threw a rabbit into Tecciztécatl's face, dimming his brilliance and turning him into the moon.\n\nTecciztécatl’s Penance embodies Coyolxauhqui’s demand for devotion and sacrifice. By offering the lives of Settlers, mortals show their unwavering faith, and the goddess responds by granting her favor in return."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_METZLIAPAN"   ;   Str = "The Aztec capital of Tenochtitlán was famously built upon an island in the center of Lake Texcoco. The name for this vast, briny body of water was Metzliapán, meaning “Moon Lake”. Beneath these waters lurk axolotls, strange and resilient amphibious creatures born from the cowardly god Xolotl, who transformed his shape and fled into the muddy depths to escape the sacrificial fires of creation."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_WINGS_OF_ITZPAPALOTL"   ;   Str = "Despite her delicate name, the Obsidian Butterfly is one of the most fearsome deities in the Aztec cosmos. She was the queen of the star demons, depicted with wings of razor-sharp obsidian blades, a skull mask, and blood-stained jaguar claws. Itzpapalotl reigns as the head of the Tzitzimime, the skeletal star demons prophesied to dive from the heavens and hunt humanity should the sun ever fail to rise."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_OBSIDIAN_KNAPPING"   ;   Str = "Elite warriors wielded the macuahuitl, a heavy wooden club lined with flaked volcanic glass. As a patron goddess of stonecutters, Itzpapalotl guided the hands of craftsmen to master the intricate art of knapping, striking obsidian into impossibly sharp prismatic blades."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_FLOWERY_WARS"   ;   Str = "The Flowery Wars were highly ritualized battles fought not for territorial conquest, but as a sacred harvest to secure live captives. By offering these rival warriors in sacrifice, the Aztecs sought to pay their cosmic debts and sustain the heavens."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_SHARDS_OF_ITZTLI"   ;   Str = "To the Mexica, obsidian was a sacred divine secretion of the gods, hidden beneath the earth. When mobilized across the vast waters of Lake Texcoco, warriors in traditional acalli (the Nahuatl word for ‘Canoe’) capped their projectiles with this volcanic glass. These missiles could effortlessly pierce through a double thickness of quilted cotton armor, securing dominion over the waterways."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_BURNT_WATER"   ;   Str = "“Burnt Water” is a reference to the Aztec concept of atl-tlachinolli, which literally translates to “water-fire” or “burning-water.” In Aztec culture, this term was a powerful difrasismo; a poetic metaphor that combined two opposing elements to create a third, distinct meaning. Specifically, the pairing of water and fire served as a sacred symbol and metaphor for war."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_TLALOQUES"   ;   Str = "The Tlaloques were a group of dwarf-like messenger gods who served as the primary assistants to Tlaloc, the supreme Aztec deity of rain, water, and storms. Representing the multiple spirits of the mountains and powerful weather phenomena, they were tasked with brewing different types of rain in vats atop mountaintops and distributing water into rivers, pools, and springs to water the world."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_TONACATEPETL"   ;   Str = "Tonacatépetl translates directly to the “Mountain of Our Sustenance”. In Aztec mythology, this was a legendary, sacred mountain that contained the seeds of all the foods humans needed to survive."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_FOUR_JARS"   ;   Str = "Tlaloc resided in a heavenly, verdant paradise called Tlalocan. Within his great house was a courtyard containing four massive jars, each corresponding to one of the four cardinal directions. Tlaloc and his dwarf-like assistants used these jars to brew and distribute different types of rain to the earth. Because his rains were so vital to the survival of the empire, Tlaloc was honored at the very center of the Mexica universe; he shared the summit of the Templo Mayor with the supreme Aztec war deity, Huitzilopochtli."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_EVENING_STAR"   ;   Str = "Xolotl and his twin brother Quetzalcoatl represent the two faces of the planet Venus. While Quetzalcoatl is the shining Morning Star, Xolotl is the dark personification of Venus as the Evening Star. In Aztec cosmology, the descent of the Evening Star signaled the sun's nightly death and its dangerous journey through the underworld. To navigate this darkness, Xolotl sends his canine avatars, the Xoloitzcuintli, to stand vigil at the empire's greatest shrines."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_STONESKIN_QUINAMETZIN"   ;   Str = "In Aztec cosmology, time is cyclical, and the world has undergone multiple eras of creation and destruction known as 'Suns'. The Mexica lived in the era of the Fifth Sun. The Quinametzin were colossal giants of the Third Sun; because they committed grave sins and refused to venerate the gods they were destroyed by a rain of volcanic fire.\n\nBy channeling his dark dominion over monsters, Xolotl calcifies the rocky flesh of his reanimated champions, to withstand heavier blows and hurl boulders with more force."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_TWISTED_LIMBS"   ;   Str = "Xolotl is the patron god of physical deformities, sickness, and monsters, and is frequently depicted in codices with profound physical deformities himself. In Aztec society, physical abnormalities were not met with the same discrimination seen in many other historical cultures. Disability was deeply integrated into their mythology and worldview, and those with physical deformities were often viewed as being favored by the gods or chosen for a special mission. By contorting the bodies of your warriors, Xolotl uses his dark power over anatomy to mutate their physical forms. He warps their limbs to give them unnatural, monstrous leg strength, transforming them into terrifying predators that jump further, dealing devastating damage."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_TORCH_OF_MISFORTUNE"   ;   Str = "In Aztec belief, the hairless Xoloitzcuintli (named after Xolotl himself) was a loyal companion in life and a necessary guide in death, bred specifically to act as a soul guide, sacrificed and buried with its master to help them safely navigate the treacherous rivers of the underworld."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_NECKLACE_OF_EYEBALLS"   ;   Str = "In Aztec codices and artwork, Mictlantecuhtli was frequently depicted as an imposing, blood-splattered skeletal figure. While his head was typically a bare skull, his eye sockets contained actual living eyeballs, intensifying his terrifying visage. To complete this fearsome appearance, Mictlantecuhtli famously wore a necklace crafted from human eyeballs, along with ear plugs made from human bones."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_OMEN_OF_DEATH"   ;   Str = "Mictlantecuhtli is the skeletal Lord of Mictlan, the lowest and northernmost realm of the Aztec underworld. He is the absolute master of the dead, governing the shadowy realm where the vast majority of souls journeyed after passing.\n\nThe Aztecs were a highly superstitious people who lived in constant fear of cosmic instability and terrifying omens. Death omens were particularly prevalent; for example, the screech of an underworld owl (tunkuluchu or chicuatli) was believed to be a direct message from Mictlan signaling imminent death."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_PRECIOUS_BONES"   ;   Str = "According to Aztec mythology, the current human race only exists because of a daring heist in the underworld. After the previous world era was destroyed, the earth was devoid of people. To populate the new world, the god Quetzalcoatl descended into Mictlan to retrieve the 'precious bones' of the people from the past era. These bones were jealously guarded by Mictlantecuhtli and his wife. After overcoming Mictlantecuhtli's tricks, Quetzalcoatl escaped with the bones, brought them to the paradise of Tamoanchan, and ground them into a powder. By bleeding upon this bone powder, the gods created the first humans of the modern age. Because of this, human bones were considered profoundly sacred, serving as the foundational building blocks of all human life."
ID = "STR_HISTORY_TECH_MICTECAH"   ;   Str = "In Aztec mythology, the Mictecah are the inhabitants of Mictlan, the land of the dead. They are deformed, entirely fleshless, skeletal beings who live in a dark, joyless, desolate, icy wasteland where the freezing wind carries razor-sharp obsidian blades and stones, blowing for all eternity, slicing and tearing the flesh from the bodies of all who passed through.\n\nOnly the Shorn Ones, the absolute highest and most feared rank of the Aztec military, could channel the terrifying nature of these underworld denizens."

Together, these enhancements turn the battlefield itself into a weapon, where ingenuity and preparation are as deadly as steel."
